Raising to 500 total (5x the big blind effectively) is a standard aggressive size here, pot-committing no one deeply while pressuring the limper and BB.

I have Qc2c in the big blind facing an isolation raise to 500 from the small blind after Claude limped. The pot is 700 and I must call 400 more (≈36% pot odds) to continue. Q2s is a weak, often dominated holding that plays very poorly multiway and out of position; against an isolating raise plus a caller I have very little realizable equity. A min-raise to 900 would be a thin, speculative bluff into two players and is not profitable long-term. Preserve stack and fold here.

Calling 400 more (500 total) with T8o from the button against an SB raise. I have adequate pot odds (1.75:1 needs ~36% equity), reasonable equity against Elon's wide SB range, and critical positional advantage for post-flop play. The combination of decent odds and position makes this a profitable call where I can exploit my button advantage and adjust to the flop dynamically.

Heads-up on the flop with Ac9c against Claude who limped-called my pre-flop raise from the button. The board Kc 7d 7c gives me the nut flush draw (four to the Ac nut flush) plus ace-high. The paired sevens make it tricky for overpairs or top pair, but Claude's limped-called range is wide: Kx, Ax (especially suited clubs), mid pairs, suited connectors, draws. As the pre-flop aggressor first to act, I should bet for value, protection (against runner-runner straights or his draws), and fold equity against marginal hands. Checking risks giving a free card to his draws or allowing check-raise bluffs. Pot bet of 1100 (full pot) builds the pot with my equity (~35-45% vs his range), pressures his 5550 stack (good SPR), and denies equity. If he shoves or raises, I have decisions but nut draw justifies calling. History shows Claude calls frequently, so value betting strong draws like this exploits that.

Calling 1100 on the K♣7♦7♣ flop with T♥8♣. I have an OESD with 8 clean outs plus additional equity from the Ten. Against Elon's SB range, I have approximately 32% equity, which exceeds the 28.5% threshold needed for 2.5:1 pot odds. My button position provides post-flop flexibility, and Elon's larger stack (16100 vs my 5550) offers good implied odds if I complete my draw. This is a profitable call with position, a strong draw, and favorable risk-reward dynamics.

On the turn Js, a relatively blank card for my nut club flush draw (9 outs remaining, ~35% equity heads-up). As preflop aggressor who pot-bet flop and got called by Claude's limped-called range (QQ+, Kx suited/clubby, 77 quads, weaker pairs/draws, Ax suited), continuing with a bet maintains initiative, denies equity to his floats/draws, extracts value from worse (overpairs fearing sets, mid Kx, worse flush draws), and applies fold equity vs marginal made hands on this paired board. Js brings possible 89 gutshots or T8 runner potential but doesn't drastically improve many villain hands. Pot is 3300, Claude has 4450 (~1.3 SPR), betting 2200 (~2/3 pot) puts pressure, builds pot for river jam if checked to us or if he flats, and sets up ICM-friendly SPR if he shoves (call with nut draw + Ac blocker). Checking risks a free river to his equity while giving him chance to check back showdown value or bluff us off. History shows Claude calls frequently but folds to continued pressure in spots (e.g., hand 51). Exploit by semi-bluffing strong draw.

FOLD - my equity doesn't justify the call. On the turn with T♥8♣ on K♣7♦7♣J♠, I have exactly 4 clean outs: the nines for a 7-8-9-T-J straight. This gives me approximately 8.7% equity on the river. To call 2200 into a 5500 pot (2.5:1 odds), I need ~29% equity to break even. My actual equity is far below this threshold. Elon's aggressive action on both the flop (1100) and turn (2200) combined with the board texture (paired sevens, king-high) strongly suggests he holds a strong hand: likely 77 (set), KK/K7 (strong pair/two pair), or another strong holding. My T and 8 kickers offer minimal additional equity against his likely range. While I had reasonable odds and position earlier in the hand, the turn brought no help and I'm now in a clearly negative expectation situation. Folding preserves my 4450 stack for better spots where I have more equity or smaller commitments.

A6o from UTG is a fold. Weak hand, terrible position, and no reason to punish myself early in the tournament. With a 4450 stack in a 150-hand session, I should wait for premium hands from better positions. Folding preserves chips for situations where I have a genuine edge.

Preflop on the button with folds to me (Claude UTG folded, implying any intermediates folded or none). Pot 150, to call 100. Ad4h is a standard button steal raise: ace blocker, decent high card potential, position advantage. Raising to 3x BB (300 total) to steal blinds or build pot in position. Opponents have shown aggression historically but defending wide vs button raises may be loose. Deep stacks allow flexibility postflop.

Elon opened to 300 from the button. I am in the small blind with A♥8♣ (Ace-eight offsuit). To call I need to put in 250 more (300 total including my posted 50) — the pot is 450 so calling is cheap and offers good immediate and implied odds. A8o is a hand I want to defend against a button open: it has top-pair potential and reasonable playability postflop. 3-betting out of position with this hand is marginal — a min-raise to 500 is unlikely to fold out better hands and will often get called, leaving me OOP; a larger 3-bet is unnecessarily large here. I will call to keep the pot manageable, see the flop, and play aggressively if I hit an Ace or strong draw, otherwise proceed cautiously vs heavy aggression.

I am in the Big Blind holding King-Seven offsuit. The Button (Elon) raised to 300, and the Small Blind (Minni) called. The pot is currently 700, and it costs me 200 more to call. This gives me pot odds of 3.5:1, or approximately 22%. Closing the action with a King-high hand in a multi-way pot at these odds is a standard call, as I only need to realize a small amount of equity to make the call profitable. I will see the flop and play cautiously out of position.

I defended preflop with A♥8♣ to see a flop, but this is a very bad flop for my hand: Q♠ Q♣ 4♦. I have no pair, no backdoor flush/straight, and very little showdown value. It’s a three-way pot (preflop opener + BB caller), so a continuation bet as a bluff is unlikely to fold out enough of their ranges and will often get called by any Q, pair, or a wide defending range. I’m out of position and don’t want to bloat the pot with essentially air. Plan: check and control the pot — if I face a meaningful bet I will fold (unless I pick up a clear price or improve), if the action checks through I get a free turn to reevaluate (and can barrel only after improving or picking up a realistic fold equity spot). Therefore: check.

On a paired board of 4d-Qs-Qc, checking is the standard move from the Big Blind after the Small Blind has already checked. My King-high has some showdown value and may even be the best hand if the original raiser missed, but there is no reason to lead into two opponents here.

Preflop raiser on the button with position. Flop QQ4 rainbow hits me for bottom pair with ace kicker and ace blocker. Paired board reduces straight/flush draws, but Qx trips possible though unlikely given preflop calls. Both opponents check, indicating weakness in their calling ranges (Ax, KQ-ish, suited connectors that missed). C-bet for value against worse Ax/pairs/overcards, protection, and to deny equity/realize fold equity. Size ~2/3 pot (600 into 900) balances value/protection while keeping pot manageable deepstacked. Checking risks giving free cards to overcards or turning into multiway pot awkwardly.

Preflop I defended with A♥8♣ which is fine, but the flop is very bad for my holding: Q♠ Q♣ 4♦. I checked as planned to control the pot and see a free card. Elon (preflop opener) now bets 600 into a ~900 pot (~66% pot). I have no pair, no realistic draw (only one heart in hand and no heart on board), am out of position, and face a bet that strongly favors a value-heavy range (trip Qs, pocket 4s, AA/KK overpairs and good Qx hands). Calling 600 into a 1500 pot requires roughly 28–29% equity to be break-even; A8o has far less vs that continuing range, and Flash still acts behind which can further complicate the spot. A bluff-raise OOP multiway is too ambitious and will rarely fold out enough better hands. With a large portion of my stack preserved (8425) and no clear path to value or equity, the correct play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

On a board of 4d-Qs-Qc, the pre-flop raiser (Elon) has bet 600 into a pot of 900. Holding only King-high (Kc 7s) with no draw, I am likely behind a wide range of hands including any pocket pair, any Ace, or any Queen. While Elon could be continuation betting with air, calling out of position with only King-high is not profitable long-term, especially given the bet size (2/3 pot) and the lack of potential for my hand to improve significantly on the turn without hitting one of my few outs.

AQs hearts is a premium hand UTG in a 4-handed game. Strong equity, suited, high cards. Standard open-raise to 3x BB (300) to build pot, isolate, take blinds if folded. Opponents' stacks allow for post-flop play; my deep stack supports aggression. No reads suggest limp or fold.

Facing an UTG open to 300, Q♣5♥ is a weak, often-dominated hand vs an early-position raising range. Calling 300 into a 450 pot requires ~40% equity to break even, which Q5o doesn’t have vs a typical UTG range, and calling risks playing a multiway pot out of position if the blinds come along. A light 3-bet/raise here with Q5o is a poor bluff candidate (weak blockers, little fold equity against a likely strong opener). With healthy stack depth I’ll conserve chips and wait for a better button spot. Fold.

I am holding Jack-Six offsuit in the Small Blind. Elon has raised from under-the-gun to 300. J6o is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against a range that is likely strong given the early position raise. The risk of being dominated or having to play a marginal hand post-flop out of position makes this an easy fold.

Calling with A7o from the big blind against Elon's UTG raise. Getting 2.25:1 pot odds and decent equity (~35-40%), with post-flop position advantage in heads-up play. The Ace provides showdown value and top-pair potential. With reasonable odds and positional advantage, calling is more profitable than folding this marginal hand.

I flopped top pair with top kicker (A7 on a 7d 2s 4s board) in a heads-up pot against Elon, who raised UTG preflop. The board is relatively dry with limited draw concerns (3 spades present). Since Elon checked the flop, I have a strong hand that needs protection and value extraction. My hand beats most unpaired holdings and draw combinations in Elon's likely UTG raising range (AK, AQ, QQ+, etc.). The 3-spade board means I should bet to protect against potential spade draws and flush draws that could complete on later streets. A bet of 350 (roughly 1/2 pot) is appropriately sized - strong enough to apply pressure on draws and weaker holdings while extracting value from marginal hands that might call. This also maintains fold equity and controls pot size for my stack depth.

Preflop raise with AQs suited was standard as a premium hand from UTG in 4-handed play, building the pot and isolating. Flop of 7d-2s-4s completely misses my range: no pair, no flush draw (hearts), no straight draw, just ace-high overcards. Claude's flat call preflop from BB suggests a wide defensive range including suited connectors, small pairs, suited aces. His flop lead for ~half pot (350 into ~650) indicates strength: likely a pair (4x-7x), set, two overcards with spade draw, or strong draw like 5s3s or 56s. Board is highly draw-heavy with open-enders (35,56) and spade flush draw. Calling commits me to a bloated pot OOP with marginal equity (~20-30% at best vs his range) and poor implied odds if I hit an A or Q, as draws complete often. Raising as a bluff is suboptimal deep-stacked against a capable defender who can call or shove his 3800 stack. Folding preserves stack for better spots; long-term EV positive given 150-hand goal and my deep stack advantage.

UTG with 3♥5♠ — a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand. I’m first to act out of 4 players with deep stacks (≈84bb); this spot requires a strong or playable hand. Limping or open‑raising here is negative EV: limp invites isolates/3‑bets from players who raise frequently, and the hand has poor postflop playability and few profitable runouts. No fold equity and poor implied odds make calling 100 unattractive. Preserve the stack for better spots — fold.

I am on the button with 9c 4s, which is a very weak hand. Standard strategy dictates folding such trash hands even in late position to avoid playing post-flop with poor equity and high risk of being dominated.

I hold 2c-4c, one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Although I'm in the small blind heads-up against the big blind (Elon), 2-4 is still effectively trash and will play poorly on most flops. The pot odds are 3:1 (150 to call 50), but hand quality matters more than pot odds with such a weak holding. Elon has a large stack (19600) and will have positional advantage postflop. Standard ABC poker dictates folding 2-4 from the SB, even heads-up, as it's better to fold the marginal chips and wait for a stronger spot. This disciplined approach has served well through 60 hands.

I am UTG in a 4-handed game with Jack-Four offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand that does not meet the requirements for an opening range from any position, especially being first to act. I will fold to avoid getting involved in a pot with a marginal hand.

5-10 offsuit is a very weak hand that doesn't justify investing chips, even from the button position. While the pot odds are reasonable (100 to call into 150) and position is favorable, this hand is at the bottom of my opening range. With only 4,750 chips and blinds at 50/100, I should be selective about which marginal hands I play. Flash has already folded, leaving just Elon (SB) and Minni (BB) to act. The risk of getting caught with weak holdings outweighs the benefit of seeing a flop. I'll preserve chips for better spots with stronger hands.

9h4d is a trash hand: offsuit, disconnected, low cards with poor playability. Preflop unraised pot, but as SB completing OOP against BB is -EV. BB likely to defend wide. Folding preserves stack for premium hands in long session (62/150). No history this hand suggests otherwise.

With A9o from UTG (early position) and a relatively short stack of 4750 chips (~47.5 BBs), this is a clear fold. In early position, I need premium holdings like premium pairs, AK, AQ, and strong broadways. A9o lacks the strength required preflop out of position, especially against aggressive opponents like Minni, Flash, and Elon. With limited chips, I want to conserve my stack for stronger situations where I have better equity. Folding preserves my resources for higher-probability spots in later streets or with premium hands.

Preflop on the button with K8o after UTG fold. Pot is 150, to call 100, likely SB limped and BB checked. K8o is a strong button raising hand offsuit due to king blocker, good high card, and excellent position. Opponents have shown tendencies to call light or defend wide from blinds based on history (e.g., Minni calls stations, Flash mixes aggression but folds to pressure). Raising isolates weak ranges, builds pot with position, and takes initiative. Standard sizing 3x BB to 300 applies pressure without overcommitting deep stacks. Folding too tight misses button equity.

I'm in the small blind with J♠7♠ facing a button open to 300. Pot is 450 and I need to add 250 (300 total) to call. J7s is a marginal but defensible hand vs a wide BTN open — suited with backdoor straight/flush potential and decent playability when I see a cheap flop. I don't want to 3-bet to 500 (or larger) because I'm out of position vs the button and J7s is too weak to take on that role profitably; folding is too nitty given the price and villain's likely wide range. I will call 250 to see the flop, plan to continue only with a clear piece of the board or strong backdoor equity, and fold to significant aggression if I miss or face a strong 3-bet.

I am in the Big Blind with 5c 2s, which is an extremely weak hand. Although I am getting decent pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 700), the hand has very poor playability post-flop and will often be dominated or have little equity. It is better to fold and wait for a stronger hand than to get involved out of position with garbage cards.

I called preflop with J♠7♠ as a defend vs a BTN open. Flop A♠ Q♣ 3♦ misses my hand — I have no pair and only remote backdoor equity (need running spades or perfect turn/river). I’m out of position against the opener and this board heavily favors their continuing range (lots of Ax/Qx and broadways). My priority is pot control: check and give up to any meaningful bet. If Elon checks back I can consider a turn stab on a favorable card (a spade or a jack/7 that improves showdown value), otherwise fold to aggression. Betting or raising OOP with this hand is concededly a losing line here.

Heads-up on the flop as preflop raiser from the button against SB caller Minni. The board is As Qc 3d, rainbow, ace-high, fairly dry. My K8o completely misses, but I have position, initiative, and king-high which has showdown value against bluffs/floats. Minni's SB defending range to BTN raise is wide: Ax (weak), Qx, suited connectors, pairs below QQ, etc. Many of these check-fold to cbet. History shows Minni calls pre light but folds to flop cbet pressure often (e.g., Hands 11,23,32,36). Standard cbet ~60% pot to deny equity, build pot if called, high fold equity. Pot 700, bet 450 (2/3 pot sizing common, applies pressure without overbetting). Deep stacks allow fold equity realization later streets if needed.

Fold. I have J♠7♠ on A♠ Q♣ 3♦ — a complete miss with only remote backdoor equity. Elon’s flop raise to 450 into a 1150 pot requires roughly 22% equity to justify a call (450 / (1150+450+450) ≈ 21.95%). My realistic equity versus his continuing range (lots of A‑x, Q‑x, broadways and some value hands) is well below that: pairing the jack or seven on the turn is ~4.3%, the chance to make a backdoor spade flush by river is ~4.2%, and overall showdown equity is in the low teens at best. I’m out of position, have poor fold equity, and a call will often commit me to later streets where I’m behind. Raising as a bluff OOP is thin and unnecessarily risky. Stick to the pre‑planned line of folding to meaningful aggression and preserve the stack for better, more favorable spots.

UTG position with 2h9h, a weak suited connector with poor playability and low equity against ranges. Facing a small to-call of 100 into 150 pot, but opening UTG requires a much stronger range to avoid playing out of position postflop. Opponents have shown aggression in past hands, so folding preserves stack for better spots in this long cash game session aiming to maximize over 150 hands.

Button with JTo (off-suit) — a standard open-raise spot. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to see the flop; limping is too passive and gives the blinds good odds to play cheaply. I want to leverage position, apply fold equity against the blinds, and avoid a multiway pot where JTo loses equity. Opponents at the table are comfortable defending but frequently open to ~300, so sizing to 300 (≈3x BB) is standard and keeps my range credible while not overcommitting from 81 BB effective. I’ll open to 300 to isolate and play postflop in position; if I face a 3-bet I can fold or proceed depending on sizing and player, but 300 maximizes short-term EV here.

I am in the Small Blind with K9o. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300. While Minni is very active and likely opening a wide range, K9o is a marginal hand to play out of position. Calling 250 more puts me in a difficult spot post-flop without the lead, and 3-betting is a high-variance play with this specific hand. Given the stack depths and the fact that I will be out of position for the entire hand, I will elect to fold and wait for a stronger opening or a better opportunity to 3-bet.

AQ of spades is a premium hand that plays well heads-up against Minni's aggressive button-raising range. Rather than just calling, I raise to 700 to build the pot with a strong hand and put immediate pressure on her. This capitalizes on my hand strength and the favorable position in a heads-up scenario where she's shown consistent aggression throughout the session.

Facing a standard BB 3-bet to 700 after my 3x open to 300. JTo on the button is a hand with decent postflop playability and position, which is valuable against a 3-bettor. Calling 400 more (700 total) gives me attractive pot odds (I only need ~28% equity to break even) and lets me use positional advantage to realize equity or apply pressure on favorable runouts. Claude’s 3-bet sizing is not huge but he can have a polarized range; 4-betting light here with JTo is too thin — I’ll either get called by better hands or face a shove where JTo performs poorly. Folding is overly passive and surrenders position and fold equity. I’ll call and proceed to play the flop in position; if Claude overpressures (large 4-bet/shove) I’ll re-evaluate and likely fold.

Continuation betting on the flop with AQs. I have two overcards, a gutshot straight draw, and a backdoor flush draw—giving me substantial equity. Minni called my pre-flop raise, likely with a medium-strength hand. Betting maintains my aggression, applies pressure, and gains fold equity. If called, I have strong drawing equity and multiple outs. The 750 bet is appropriately sized at ~52% of the current pot.

I have JdTs on a 7♦ J♣ T♦ flop — I’ve flopped top two pair. Claude 3‑bet me preflop and now bets 750 into a 2200 pot, so his range is polarized (overpairs, broadways, some pairs/sets). Very few hands beat me here (mainly sets: 77/TT/JJ); most of his range is behind (AA/KK/QQ, AK, AQ, single J/T combos, bluffs). The board also contains two diamonds, so there are diamond draws that I want to charge. I should be building the pot for value and protecting against draws rather than playing passively. A raise is the correct line: it extracts value from worse pairs/overpairs and charges draws while keeping sets and very strong hands committed or able to reveal themselves. I’ll make the minimum meaningful raise to 1500 total (keeps worse hands and overpairs in, still applies pressure), then re‑evaluate on the turn if Claude jams or over‑raises. Action: raise to 1500 (total chips committed).

I hold AQs, which is a strong hand with multiple equity sources against Minni's aggressive play. On this 7d-Jc-Td flop, I have: two overcards (both aces and queens beat most hands), a gutshot straight draw (any K completes a wheel), and a backdoor flush draw. Minni's check-raise from 750 to 1500 is aggressive, but my hand has approximately 40-45% equity against a wide range of her possible holdings (medium pairs, broadway cards, or even a bluff). The pot odds are excellent at nearly 6:1 (3700 to call 750). Raising here would commit me to ~2250, leaving only 1050 chips and forcing me into a marginal spot where I'm essentially committed anyway. By calling, I see the turn while maintaining flexibility and improving on many runouts. My current stack of 3300 supports a call without overcommitting, allowing me to make a more informed decision on the turn based on new information.

Minni check-raises aggressively on the flop with AcJs-Td, but now checks on the turn after the 3s comes (a relatively neutral card). This check suggests weakness or a take-a-cautious-line hand. I hold AQs, one of the strongest hands in this spot with substantial equity. My hand has approximately 16+ clean outs: three Aces, three Queens, four Kings (making a Broadway straight), and six additional spades for the flush (avoiding double-counting). This gives me roughly 35-40% equity against her range. The pot is 4450 with only 2550 remaining in my stack. If I bet, I risk pushing myself into a pot-committed situation with 1+ cards to come. Minni's check-back line is suspicious—she may have a medium-strength hand (medium pair, Broadway cards) that she's not comfortable playing in a larger pot. By checking back, I see the river for free with strong equity. This preserves my flexibility: if I hit my outs (likely ~35% of the time), I'll have a strong value hand. If I miss, I can fold to a river bet without further loss. Checking is the optimal play for pot control and maximizing expected value with a draw-heavy hand one card from the end.

I have top two pair (J-T) on a J♣ T♦ 7♦ - 3♠ board. Pot is 4,450 and Claude checked to me on the turn after calling my flop raise. This is a clear value-bet spot: my hand is ahead of most of his calling range (worse Jx, Tx, 7x and many broadway/gutter draws) while only a few combos (sets: 77/TT/JJ) beat me — and those set combos are quite limited because I hold J♦ and T♠ (pocket JJ/TT combos are reduced). Checking gives free cards to straight/runner draws; betting denies equity and extracts value. I’ll size to roughly half-pot (2,200) — big enough to get calls from worse and charge Q9/KQ style straights, but not so huge that I fold out too many worse hands or unnecessarily overcommit. If Claude jams his remaining ~2,550 I’m comfortable calling given the relatively small number of set combos in his range and the value I’ll get from the hands that call. Action: bet 2,200.

Calling is mathematically sound. With 16 outs (roughly 34% equity) and 3:1 pot odds, I need only 25% equity to break even. My strong draw with overcards, gutshot straight, and backdoor flush has sufficient equity to justify the call. While this commits nearly my entire stack (350 remaining), the +EV nature of the decision and the tournament structure support playing this spot. The hand will be decided on the river with chips on the line, but the odds favor the call over folding away equity.

On the river with board 7d-Jc-Td-3s-4c, I hold AQs which gives me ace-high (A-Q-J-T-7). I have no made hand—no pair, no straight (would need K), no flush (only 3 spades total). Minni has checked to me. Given the action pattern (aggressive check-raises on flop and turn, now checking on river), she likely has either: (1) a hand she's satisfied with that will call a bet, or (2) a hand that completely whiffed that will fold anyway. With only 350 chips remaining and an 8850 chip pot, betting accomplishes little—I can't fold out many made hands that beat me, and any hand that would fold has already folded. Checking allows me to reach showdown with my ace-high, which has some value and might win against draws or hands worse than top pair. This preserves chip efficiency and maximizes expected value in a showdown-bound spot where I have marginal equity.

I have two pair (Jacks and Tens) on a fairly dry 7♦ J♣ T♦ 3♠ 4♣ river. Very few hands beat me (mainly sets: 77/TT/JJ); Claude’s line (preflop 3‑bet, flop bet and call of my raise, turn call) contains many worse Jx/Tx/7x and some overpairs that will pay to see a small river bet. He’s left with only 350, so I want to extract value without overcommitting — a small bet will get called by most worse hands while keeping my risk limited vs the few set combos. I’ll bet 300 to maximize thin value; if he jams his remaining 350 I’ll call because the number of combos that beat me is small and the pot odds justify a call.

Folding ace-high on the river. While pot odds are 30:1 (requiring only 3.2% equity to break even), my actual equity against Minni's river-raising range is only 2-5%. She's showing strength through her betting pattern (check-raise flop, check turn, raise river), likely indicating a made hand. With 350 chips remaining and 86 hands left to play, preserving my final 50 chips for better spots is more valuable than calling with marginal equity. This is a disciplined fold in a +EV session context.

UTG with 9♠2♦ — a junk hand with almost no playability. Out of position against three opponents (button, short SB and deep BB) this hand is dominated by most calling/raising ranges and has poor equity and blocker value. Calling 100 to see a flop creates a multiway OOP pot with little implied odds; raising to try and steal risks facing a shove from the short-stacked SB or a 3-bet from deep players. Long-term EV is best preserved by folding and waiting for a much stronger UTG-opening hand.

I am on the button with Ace-Nine offsuit. This is a standard opening hand in this position when the action has folded to me. I'll raise to 300, which effectively puts Claude in the Small Blind all-in as he only has 300 chips left. A9o is strong enough to play against both blinds, especially given Claude's short stack.

Short-stacked situation with Th-Qc. Calling 250 leaves only 50 chips, but the hand has decent equity against Flash's button raise range. As a broadway hand, ThQc is worth committing to here. Rather than fold and waste the SB, I'll go ahead with this marginal-to-decent hand and hope to win to rebuild my stack.

2h7h is a very weak suited hand, especially out of position in the big blind facing a button raise from Flash and a call from Claude in the small blind. Pot odds are about 3.5:1 (200 to call into 900), requiring roughly 18% equity, but 27s has poor playability postflop OOP and is dominated by better suited hands or connectors in villains' wide ranges. Claude's tiny remaining stack (50) adds dead money, but doesn't sufficiently improve equity or postflop prospects against two opponents. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots in a long session (hand 65/150).

Short-stacked all-in shove with remaining chips. T-Q has Q-kicker to the 9-9 board and reasonable equity against Flash's wide button raising range. Already committed 250 into 700 pot; pushing remaining 50 gives best chance to improve or win at showdown rather than folding the SB.

I have flopped trips (nines) with an Ace kicker on a 9-9-8 board. This is a very strong hand. Claude called my pre-flop raise and appears to be effectively all-in or nearly all-in. Since I have a monster hand and the amount to call is negligible (50), I will call to proceed to the next street and eventual showdown. My goal is simply to realize the equity of my trips.

I am in the UTG position with Ace-Eight offsuit. Claude is on the button with a very short stack (8 BBs), while Elon and Minni are deep. My hand is strong enough to open for a standard raise in a 4-handed game, especially with a short stack behind me that I might be able to isolate or force to commit with a wider range. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and exert pressure.

With Q-2o (Queen-high with a deuce kicker) on the button, this is a weak holding that doesn't warrant a call despite my short stack of 800 chips. While I'm not desperately short, I need to be selective with my push-fold hands. Q-2o is well below the threshold of hands I should commit chips with, especially with Flash showing strength from UTG and two strong opponents (Elon and Minni) still to act behind me. I have enough chips remaining to wait for a better spot with a premium hand. Folding preserves my stack for a more favorable situation in the coming hands where I can make a stand with actual value.

83o is an extremely weak hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card value. Facing an UTG raise from Flash, who likely has a strong range, defending from the SB OOP is disastrous. Pot odds are poor (250 to call into 450 pot ~36% implied, but hand has negligible equity). Immediate fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Facing an UTG open to 300 in a 4-handed game, my 3♠4♥ is a very marginal hand: dominated by a large portion of an UTG raising range and has poor post‑flop playability out of position. The pot is 450 and I must call 200 more (total 300), which requires ~23–24% equity to break even. Versus a reasonable UTG open my actual equity is likely below that, and any small raise (min‑raise to 500) is unlikely to fold enough of his range to be profitable and commits me to costly postflop spots with a weak hand. I’ll fold and preserve my stack for better spots over the 150‑hand session (the 100 BB already posted is a sunk cost).

I'm in UTG with T♠K♥, a solid broadway hand that plays well in early position. My stack is short (800 chips), which actually gives me a good opportunity to play aggressively with this quality hand. Raising to 400 (approximately 4x the BB) accomplishes several things: (1) it builds a pot where my hand has good equity, (2) it creates fold equity against weaker holdings, and (3) with my short stack, I'm getting into favorable risk/reward territory. This hand is well above the UTG raising threshold, and aggressive play with broadway cards from early position is standard poker strategy. The raise also prevents me from getting drawn out on by allowing opponents to cheaply see flops, which is important when stack equity is at a premium.

Q2 offsuit is a very weak hand with no high card value, suitedness, or connectivity. Facing an UTG raise from Claude to 400 into a 550 pot, his range is strong (premium hands like pairs, AK, AQ+). Even on the button with deep stacks, calling or raising is -EV as it plays poorly postflop OOP most of the time and vulnerable to 3-bets. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this long 150-hand game.

Claude has only 400 chips and his 400 open is effectively an all‑in from UTG (4BB). I need to put 350 more (total 400 committed) to call; that would make the pot ~900, so I need roughly 350/900 ≈ 39% equity to break even heads‑up. Short stacks at this depth shove very wide, so K♦5♣ has reasonable equity vs a wide push and the price is cheap relative to my 12.5k stack. I won’t raise to 700 — that just risks more and invites a squeeze from the BB with a marginal holding. I’ll call the 400 total to see the flop and re‑evaluate if Flash also comes along or puts pressure postflop.

I am in the Big Blind with Jd 2d. Claude has raised to 400 from UTG, but notably, Claude only has 400 total chips at the start of the hand (based on the provided status, although the pot/stack math looks a bit inconsistent, I must assume he is all-in or very close to it). Minni has called the 400. I need to call 300 more to see a flop in a pot that will be at least 1200. J2 suited is not a strong hand, but with the pot odds offered (300 to call into a 900 pot) and the fact that Claude is likely all-in or nearly so, closing the action and seeing a flop with a suited hand is reasonable. However, if Claude only has 400 chips, this is essentially a three-way pot where I'm getting great odds.

I have K♦5♣ on K♠6♥4♣ — top pair with a weak kicker. Claude is already all‑in for 400 and can't change the action; Flash is the only opponent with chips who can bet/raise. The board is very dry (no real straights or flushes), so my hand is likely ahead of much of Flash's calling range (broadways, weaker pairs, Kx). Out of position I prefer to extract value and deny free cards rather than checking and giving opponents a free turn. A half‑pot value bet (~600 into a 1200 pot) will get called by worse Kx and medium pairs, keeps the pot controlled if I face aggression, and prices out marginal floats. If Flash check‑raises big I will re‑evaluate (my kicker is weak so I can fold to a large line); for now bet 600 for thin value and protection.

I have Jack-high with no draws on a King-high board. Minni has led out for 600 into a pot where Claude is likely already all-in. There is no reason to continue with this hand as I have no equity and very little chance of winning the pot even if I were to bluff later, given that Minni is showing strength into an all-in player.

Top pair with T kicker on K-6-4 flop against Minni's raise. I'm getting 4.5:1 odds and have approximately 40-45% equity against her likely range. With a 400-chip stack remaining, I need to take favorable spots where my equity is strong relative to the pot odds. Folding would abandon chips unnecessarily when I have a playable hand and excellent odds. This is a standard all-in call with a short stack.

Button with A♣4♦ in a three-handed spot — this is a clear open-raise opportunity. I want to take the initiative and apply pressure to the blinds rather than just calling 100. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3bb) — bigger than the min-raise to get more fold equity and avoid giving Elon cheap odds to see a flop. If one or both call I can use position to c-bet favorable flops, control the pot on dry boards, and look to realize my equity (pairing the Ace or backdoors). If I face a sizable 3‑bet, A4o is typically a fold — I’ll preserve stack equity against strong ranges.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Trey offsuit. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. Q3o is a very weak hand to defend from the Small Blind, especially since I would be out of position for the rest of the hand against an active player. Standard strategy dictates a fold here to conserve chips for better opportunities.

Q3o is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability postflop, low equity against a button raiser's wide range, and easily dominated by Qx or pair hands. Defending this OOP would lead to difficult spots and negative EV long-term. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities, especially deep-stacked at 198x BB.

I am on the button with 8h 4s, which is a very weak hand. The action is on me. I should typically fold this hand in almost any position, especially when facing no action yet. Folding is the most standard and profitable play with such low equity.

Js4h is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability out of position. Standard play when folded to in the small blind vs big blind is to raise a wide range or fold trash like this; limping is poor strategy as it invites trouble OOP postflop. J4o lacks the equity, blockers, or connectivity to justify calling the additional 50 (3:1 implied but dominated risks) or min-raising for a steal against an unknown BB defense range from Minni, who has shown aggression in history. Deep stacks don't change this being a clear fold to preserve stack for premium spots. Over 150 hands, folding marginals maximizes EV.

3-handed on the button with 67o, a speculative offsuit connector. Deep stacks, excellent position. Standard to open-raise a wide range (45-55%+) from button 3-handed to steal blinds and build pot with position. 67o has good playability, can flop straights/flushes/draws. Opponents Minni and Flash have shown aggression but also folds to raises. Raising to 3x BB (300) is standard size. Folding too tight; limping bad OOP potential. Pot 150, minraise 200, but 300 balanced.

Elon opened to 300 on the button and I’m in the small blind with Q♥9♥. This is a marginal but playable suited queen with decent post‑flop playability and backdoor heart equity. 3‑betting here is thin — I’d be out of position to the button and could still face a cold‑call or squeeze from the big blind (Flash); raising to isolate is risky and often dominated by Elon’s button range. I’ll defend by calling 250 more (300 total) to see a flop cheaply, preserve stack flexibility, and evaluate flop texture. If I face a large 3‑bet preflop I’ll likely fold; if we see a flop I’ll proceed based on texture (look to play hearts/Q‑high boards more aggressively, otherwise proceed cautiously OOP).

I am in the big blind with Jack-Five offsuit. The button has raised to 300 and the small blind has called. While the pot odds are somewhat attractive (200 to call into a pot of 700), J5o is a very weak hand to play out of position in a three-way pot. It is easily dominated by the raising ranges of the button and the calling range of the small blind, making it difficult to realize equity on most flops. Folding is the most prudent long-term strategy here to preserve my stack.

Flop: Jh 8s Kc. I’ve got Qh9h — no pair, just Q‑high with a weak backdoor heart possibility and a straight possibility if a Ten shows up. Elon opened from the button, so his range will include lots of Kx/Jx/ broadways that dominate me. Out of position I have very little fold equity and a c‑bet here will frequently be called by better hands, so I should control the pot and realize equity. Rough improvement odds: ~16.4% to hit a Ten by the river, ~12.5% to pair my Queen by the river, ~4.2% to make a heart flush by river (runner‑runner). Plan: check and evaluate. If Elon checks back I get a free turn card to improve; if he bets a small/medium size (<= ~pot) I’ll consider calling to try to hit my outs; if he fires a large or polarizing bet I’ll fold OOP with this marginal holding.

3-handed, button vs SB after calling my raise. Flop Kc Jh 8s is somewhat drawy but rainbow. My 67o missed completely - no pair, no strong draw, just air with backdoor SPDS (spade? no, 7h6c). As preflop raiser with wide button range including Ax, Kx, broadway, pairs, this flop hits my range decently (KJ, KT, AJ, K8 etc.). Minni checked SB, which is standard but shows no strong made hand likely. Deep stacks, position advantage postflop. C-bet sizing ~60% pot (400-450) to fold out her weaker pairs/draws/ace-high, deny equity, or get called by worse. Checking risks giving free cards to her draws (Q9, T9 possible) and realizes zero equity. Standard continuation bet here for thin value/protection and as bluff since range advantage.

I checked the flop OOP with Q♥9♥ and Elon fires a small/medium C‑bet (450 into a 1,150 pot). I’m behind a lot of his realistic continuing range (Kx, Jx, broadways) but the sizing gives good pot odds to chase. Naive outs: 4 Tens + 3 Queens + 3 Nines = 10 clean outs to make a pair/straight by river (plus tiny runner‑runner heart flush chance) → roughly 38% to improve to at least a pair/straight by the river. Calling 450 into a ~2,050 pot after my call requires ≈22% equity, so this is +EV to see the turn. Raising OOP to the min (900) is too thin and would often be dominated or face a shove; I’ll keep the pot controlled and realise equity. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to large turn aggression if I don’t improve, and if I hit a Queen/Ten (or a very favorable runout) I’ll proceed more aggressively.

Turn paired the J (Jh...Js). I hold Qh9h — no pair and my runner‑runner heart chance is dead. With two jacks on board Elon’s continuing range is heavily weighted to strong hands (Jx trips, Kx, KJ, strong broadways) that dominate me. I have ~10 nominal outs to improve to a pair/straight on the river (≈10/46 ≈ 21.7%), but many of those are non‑clean against Kx/Jx. Out of position with essentially no fold equity, the correct play is to control the pot and see a free river: check. Plan: if Elon checks back I get one river card to improve; if he bets, fold to meaningful pressure and only consider calling a very small probe (≈≤1/4–1/3 pot) because the raw equity is ~22%.

Turn board pairs the jack (Jh Js 8s Kc), making straights possible with QT/T9 but killing some of my missed draws and improving Jx hands in both ranges to two pair or trips. My 67h c has zero equity - no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw (67 needs 5-9 for straight, board has 8 but no 5/9), just backdoors that are irrelevant. Minni check-called flop as SB defender vs my wide button range; her check-call suggests medium strength like Kx (K8-KT, KQ), weak Jx, 98/87 sets, or stubborn Ax/draws that called. Her turn check is weak - unlikely monster (KK/88/KJ she'd bet for value/protection), more likely giving up with missed draws or trapping weakly. But paired board favors her calling range over mine; my bluffs like QTo, 97s missed harder. Position great, but deep stacks (SPR ~8 into 1600 pot), betting risks getting called/raised by Jx/Kx while I have air, spewing chips. Checking realizes 0 equity safely, controls pot, sees river cheap/free, reps strength (c-betting flop then checking turn looks like strong slowplayed hand like KK/JJ/set), and sets up river bluffs if check-back and scary card. Bluffing turn check too thin - she checks twice, pot grows unnecessarily with nothing. Standard check-behind to pot control and avoid variance.

Board: Jh 8s Kc Js Ah; my hand Qh9h — I have no pair and missed my backdoor heart/queen. Elon opened and c‑bet the flop then checked the turn; that line is consistent with both pot‑control with a strong pair (Kx, Ax) or a slow/medium hand, and the river Ace only improves his continuing range. I have essentially zero showdown value and very little fold equity — a bluff here would only fold the absolute air but will get called by Kx/Jx/Ax and many broadway pairs. With the pot already 1600 and deep stacks, the prudent +EV play is to keep the pot small and concede — check and fold to any meaningful river bet. If Elon checks back I’ll take the free showdown card (accepting the likely loss); if he bets I’ll fold unless it’s an absurdly tiny probe that’s cheaper than the chance to see a showdown (which is unlikely).

River board pairs the Js on turn, completing straights like QT/T9 and improving Jx to two-pair/trips, then Ah brings possible ace-high, top pair, or weak flush draws. My 6c7h has zero equity: no pair, no flush (only 7h backdoor), no straight possible. Minni's range for SB call pre, check-call flop c-bet (vs my wide button range): favors Kx (top pair kings), weaker Jx, sets, stubborn draws (spades/hearts), some Ax. Her turn check after board pairs suggests not super strong (would bet two-pair/trips/sets for value/protection), more likely medium like mid Kx, missed draw, or trap. Triple check OOP on river indicates caution, likely showdown value but afraid of my range (AJ/AK/AQ, KJ, sets, KK/JJ). My line (preflop raise, flop cbet, turn check-behind) reps polarization: strong slowplay (sets/KJ/AJ) or bluffs that missed. However, bluffing ~70% pot risks call/raise from her Kx/Jx value, spewing ~1000+ chips into deep effective stacks (her 13k). History shows Minni check-calls river reasonably often (e.g., Hand 1,4,8,13,21,41,44,54). No fold equity reliable vs her medium strength; checking preserves stack, realizes 0 EV safely, avoids variance in marathon 150-hand game. Optimal pot control with air after turn check.

I’m on the button with Q♦8♦ — a playable, suited queen with good postflop playability and reasonable blocker value. There are only the two blinds to beat, stacks are deep, and the table’s standard open size has been 300 repeatedly. I want to take the initiative, apply pressure to the small blind’s wide defending range, and avoid giving cheap flops. A raise to 300 (≈3x the big blind) gives good fold equity while keeping the price reasonable if called. If I face a sizeable 3‑bet I’ll presume a stronger range and fold, but in position I can continue or realize equity against calls. Action: raise to 300.

In a 3-handed game, the button opening range is typically very wide. While Ace-Trey offsuit is ahead of some of that range, it plays poorly out of position, especially against a deep-stacked and aggressive player like Minni. Calling from the small blind is generally a sub-optimal strategy as it invites a squeeze from the big blind and leaves me playing the rest of the hand out of position. Given the stack sizes and the opponent's tendency to play back at 3-bets, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Hand J4o is a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and playability, especially out of position in the big blind. Minni's button raise indicates a wide range, but it still dominates this hand with low equity and reverse implied odds. Pot odds of calling 200 into 450 (2.25:1) are insufficient given multi-street EV loss OOP. Folding preserves stack for premium spots in a long 150-hand game.

I am on the button with 3h 2s, which is the weakest possible starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Given the 3-handed nature of the game and the fact that both opponents have significantly larger stacks, there is no strategic value in attempting a steal or entering the pot with this hand. Folding is the optimal play to preserve my chip stack for a better opportunity.

Q2 offsuit is a marginal hand at best from the small blind. It has poor equity against a big blind defending range, low playability postflop (queen-high with a deuce kicker, offsuit), and minimal high card strength. Flash has folded, leaving us heads-up vs Minni who has shown aggression in the past but also folds to raises. Completing adds dead money but risks tough spots OOP with a trash hand. Standard play is to fold weak hands like this in SB to preserve stack for premium opportunities over 150 hands.

3-handed on the button with 56s suited connectors, excellent speculative hand for multiway or heads-up pots in position. SB folded, facing BB with 60BB stack. Standard to raise to 3x BB (300) to take initiative, build pot, and leverage position post-flop. Folding too tight, limping passive. Opponent ranges wide in BB, but we have good playability.

Elon opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the small blind with A♣8♦ — too good to fold but vulnerable playing a multiway pot OOP. I want to take the initiative and isolate the button rather than calling and likely getting into a 3‑way pot with Flash. A 3‑bet to 900 (≈3x the open) gives meaningful fold equity vs the BB, keeps the size reasonable vs Elon, and leverages the ace-blocker while leaving plenty of deep-stack postflop room. Plan: fold to a large 4‑bet, proceed heads‑up postflop if Elon calls, and continue aggression with initiative.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ten of Diamonds and Nine of Clubs. Elon raised from the Button, and Minni 3-bet from the Small Blind to 900. T9 offsuit is a weak hand to defend against a 3-bet, especially when sandwiched between two players with significant stacks. Minni's 3-bet range is likely much stronger than my holdings, and I would be playing the rest of the hand out of position. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

Facing a 3-bet from Minni in the SB after my button open-raise with 56s suited connectors. Excellent speculative hand with great playability, straight/flush potential, position advantage heads-up. Pot odds are favorable: calling 600 more into ~1300 pot (pot odds ~3.2:1), implied odds strong with deep effective stacks (~100BB+). Minni's history shows aggression (frequent raises/3-bets), so range polarized - value (QQ+, AK) or bluffs. Calling keeps pot manageable, realizes equity post-flop in position where we can outplay. Raising too thin without fold equity given depths; folding too tight for this spot. Standard call.

I was the preflop aggressor and we’re heads‑up on a fairly dry Q♠9♦6♦ flop. I have A♣8♦ — no pair but an Ace blocker and a single diamond; this hand is ideal for a continuation bet to capitalize on fold equity and to deny equity to many of Elon’s calling hands (broadways, Kx, some weaker Axs). A modest c‑bet ~40% pot (≈800 into 1900) applies pressure while keeping the pot manageable with deep stacks. Plan: take the pot down now vs his weaker holdings; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (check behind vs heavy action or barre l selectively), and fold to significant aggression if he shows clear strength.

Flop of Qs9d6d with 56s: I flop bottom pair (pair of sixes with 6s over 6d), nut spade flush draw (10 spade outs remaining), and minor straight draw potential (gutshot to 7 for wheelish or better combos). Extremely strong hand with huge equity (~35-45% vs Minni's polarized 3-bet/c-bet range: QQ+/AK/AQ/Q9s/set mining/99 but capped no sets likely, bluffs like Ax suited/dominating connectors). Pot odds excellent: 800 to call into 2700 (~3.4:1, need ~23% equity which we crush). Position allows controlling pot OOP no I'm IP heads-up, realizing equity by calling small bet (44% pot), outplaying turns/rivers. Raising risks bloating pot OOP no, but stacks deep (eff ~130BB), implied odds massive if we hit. Minni aggressive history (frequent 3bets/cbets), so range includes bluffs we snap; folding impossible, raising thin without max fold equity. Standard disciplined call to pot control and extract max value long-term.

Turn pairs the 6 (Qs 9d 6d 6c). I hold A♣8♦ — I missed the flop and the backdoor diamond draw is effectively dead on the turn. Elon called my flop c-bet, so his calling range is weighted toward Qx/9x, pocket pairs and some made hands that are now improved or have decent showdown value. Betting here would be a thin bluff that gets called too often and unnecessarily bloats a pot out of position. I’ll control the pot and check to evaluate river action — fold to significant aggression on the turn, and look for a better spot or a blank river to bluff later.

Turn board Qs9d6d6c pairs the sixes, giving me trip sixes (6s with board's 6d6c) plus the nut spade flush draw (Qs on board, my 5s6s). Monster hand with ~90%+ equity vs. villain's range. Preflop 3-bet range from SB (QQ+, AK, suited broadways, Ax suited, some suited connectors), flop c-bet 800 into ~1900 (42% pot), called. Turn check after pairing low board suggests caution: possible top pair Qx good kicker pot control, 99,77, draws slowing down, weaker Ax/AQ missed, or traps with sets/full houses unlikely (99/66 too narrow pre). IP with deep stacks (eff ~130BB), betting for value from Qx,99, Tx9x,77,88, diamond draws, missed Ax. Check-back misses value and allows cheap river completion. Size 2/3 pot (2333→2500 round) extracts from worse, sets up river overbet/shove if called, thin value vs cautious range, fold equity vs pure air. Her aggressive history (frequent 3bets/cbets) but check indicates weakness. Cannot check; must bet.

I opened/3‑bet preflop and c‑bet the dry Q♠9♦6♦ flop with A♣8♦ to take it down. The turn (6♣) killed my tiny backdoor diamond hope and I checked to control the pot. Elon now bets 2,500 into the pot (pot is 6,000; I must call 2,500 to win a final ~8,500), so the call requires ~29% equity. With only Ace‑high (three A outs on the river) and no live draws, my true equity versus his calling/raising range (Qx, 9x, pocket pairs, and some strong combos) is well below that. I’m out of position and would face tough river decisions or further pressure even if I call. Per my turn plan to fold to significant aggression and to preserve stack for better spots, this is a clear fold.

I have Q♠J♠ on the button in a 3‑handed pot with stacks deep (~132bb me, 59bb SB, 206bb BB). This is a strong, playable suited Broadway in position with good equity and post‑flop playability. My table image has been opening around 300; making a standard 3xBB open (to 300 total) maximizes fold equity against the small blind, isolates heads‑up or leaves me last to act postflop, and keeps the pot size manageable against Elon's deep stack. If Elon 3‑bets small I’ll consider calling and playing postflop; against a large 3‑bet I’ll re‑evaluate and often fold to avoid bloating the pot out of position. If called, plan is to c‑bet favorable flop textures and control the pot on dry boards.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Four offsuit. The Button (Minni) has opened with a standard raise. Q4o is a very weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive opener, and there is still the Big Blind (Elon) left to act. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

Pocket sevens is a strong hand facing a button open-raise from Minni, who raises frequently and widely from late position based on hand history. Flash has folded SB, so heads-up vs Minni's deep stack. Excellent pot odds to call (200 into 450), but 77 plays well postflop and wants to charge drawing hands or worse pairs. 3-betting to 900 (3x her raise) for value, to build pot, deny equity to Ax, Kx, suited connectors, and take initiative. Deep stacks allow for set-mining if called, but prefer to isolate now. Minni's history shows she calls 3-bets occasionally with marginal hands.

Call to 900 total. Q♠J♠ on the button is a clear call vs Elon's 3‑bet to 900: I have position, deep stacks, and a hand with good equity and post‑flop playability. The pot is 1,250 and I must add 600, so I need ~32% equity (600/(1,250+600)) — QJs does well enough versus a typical BB 3‑bet range (AK/AQ, broadways, some suited connectors/pairs). A 4‑bet would polarize me to very strong hands or pure bluffs; QJs is not a reliable value 4‑bet and a small 4‑bet won't buy enough folds. Plan is to play in position: c‑bet on favourable (spade/connected/Q/J) flops, control pot size or check fold on A/K‑heavy dry boards, and extract value when I make a strong hand or big draw.

Pocket 7s remains strong as an overpair to the low board cards (6-2) but is vulnerable to the Ac, which caps top pair and hits Minni's button calling range vs my 3-bet hard (Ax wide, including suited aces). The board has a flush draw (two clubs) and is fairly dry otherwise (no straight draws really). As preflop 3-bettor OOP (BB acts first postflop), continuing with a c-bet protects against overcards turning or flush completing, charges draws (club flush draws, backdoor stuff), and extracts value from worse pairs (55,44,33,22, bluffs, floats). Minni's history shows she calls bets with marginal hands and checks often. Bet sizing ~50% pot (900 into 1850) balances value/protection without overbetting deep stacks. Check would allow free cards to draws or pot control, but aggression builds pot when ahead vs her wide range portions (non-Ax pairs, KQ, suited connectors she flats with). Folding equity minor but present.

Call 900. Facing Elon's flop c‑bet on A♣6♣2♦, Q♠J♠ has no pair but reasonable equity and — most importantly — position. I only need ~900/(2750+900) ≈ 24.7% equity to continue, and against a typical BB 3‑bet/c‑bet range QJ has enough chance to realize equity or to float and take the pot away on a safe turn. Raising as a bluff here is too thin with an Ace on board (Elon’s range is heavy in Ax and strong broadways), and folding would surrender too much equity for a small price relative to my stack. Plan: call and evaluate the turn — press if I improve (pair Q/J) or the turn is a neutral card that favours a bluff/float line; be prepared to fold to heavy turn aggression on obvious Ace/club/King bricks.

Pocket 7s remains an overpair on a board of 6c-2d-Ac-8s, ahead of most non-Ax hands in Minni's flatting range (pairs below A like 88-TT blocked somewhat, 55-66 sets unlikely given preflop action, draws). However, Ac hits her button calling range vs my 3-bet heavily (AJo+, suited Ax wide). Turn 8s is a neutral card: brings spade draw but doesn't complete club flush draw or strong straights. As OOP aggressor, continuation bet continues to deny equity to draws (club/spade flush draws, backdoor), extract value from trapped pairs/bluffs/floats, and build pot when ahead. Her history shows frequent calls with marginal holdings postflop. Pot 3650, bet ~50% pot (1800) balances value/protection without committing too much deep (~5.5 SPR post-bet). Checking risks giving free card to draws or allowing pot control only if she bets big (fold equity low anyway). Aggression aligns with preflop/flop lines to represent AA/AK/AQ.

Call 1800. I have Q♠J♠ (two overcards) on A♣-6c-2d-8s — no pair but good equity to a Q or J on the river (6 outs ≈ 26%). The turn bet is small relative to the pot (need ~1800/(5450+1800) ≈ 24.8% equity), so a call is justified by pot odds and my positional advantage. Elon’s line (preflop 3‑bet, flop c‑bet, turn barrel) is heavily weighted toward A‑x, strong broadways and some value pairs, but also contains bluffs — calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me realize equity and use position on the river. Raising here would overpolarize me with no pair and little fold equity vs a range that frequently contains Ax; folding surrenders correct pot odds. Plan: call to see river, value/raise when I pair Q/J or extract against weaker holdings, and fold to large river jams when I miss and face decisive aggression.

Pocket 7s on this river board (6c-2d-Ac-8s-9s) is an overpair but vulnerable to the dominant Ax hands in Minni's flatting range versus my preflop 3-bet, which she has called on every street including sizable flop (900 into ~1900) and turn (1800 into ~3700) bets on an Ace-high board. The river 9s is a marginal card: completes no flush (only two spades now, spade draw still live but thin), potentially helps T7/JT straight (unlikely in her precise calling range), or backdoor draws, but doesn't improve many weaker hands like 88 (pairs board) or lower pairs. As consistent aggressor OOP, my range is capped below AA/AK/AQ/Ax, but 77 blocks some sets and is ahead of pure bluffs/floats/draws she might have called with lightly. However, betting risks value-raise from Ax or call from better pairs/two-pair/straights, while pot odds for her would be great to call any bet. SPR low (~1.2 post-turn), but checking induces bluffs from missed draws (spade/club), extracts thin value from trapped worse (unlikely), or gets to showdown cheaply where 77 has ~30-40% equity vs her top Ax-heavy range (per history of calling marginal but folding to aggression sometimes). Her history shows check-calls but not frequent river barrels OOP; checking exploits by denying her fold equity on bluffs and avoiding tough spots vs better. Long-term EV positive vs aggressive button who flats wide but folds to consistent pressure.

I have Q♠J♠ and missed — no pair and no flush (board A♣-6c-2d-8s-9s). Elon’s line (preflop 3‑bet, flop c‑bet, turn barrel) is heavily weighted toward value (Ax, strong broadways, pairs); his river check is consistent with pot‑control with an Ace or a slow‑play/induce check with a very strong hand, and also with some missed bluffs. Betting here would be a pure bluff and would require a very large size to fold out most Ax and strong broadways; my Q/J blockers reduce some combos but not enough to generate reliable fold equity. With 7,250 in the pot and 9,675 behind, I don’t want to overcommit on a low‑equity bluff — lowest variance / highest EV line is to check and go to showdown. I’ll check and concede unless Elon shows down a worse hand or folds.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with Queen-Trey offsuit. While button opening ranges are typically wide in short-handed play, Q3o is far too weak to justify a raise, especially with active opponents in the blinds. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Preflop in SB vs BB unraised pot. Holding 9h2h, a weak suited gapper with poor post-flop playability OOP. Pot odds are favorable (calling 50 into 150, ~3.5:1), but BB (Minni) defends wide and is aggressive based on history. Equity realization low with this hand out of position; better to preserve stack for premium spots in deep-stack cash game. Standard fold to avoid tough post-flop decisions.

Pocket Kings is one of the strongest preflop hands, second only to AA. Sitting on the button with excellent position and deep stacks against the blinds. The pot is unraised (standard blinds posted, pot 150, to call 100), so this is a standard open-raise spot to build the pot, apply pressure on the blinds, and thin the field while getting value from worse hands. Raising to 300 (3x the BB) is a standard sizing for button opens in 3-handed play, balancing isolation and pot-building without overcommitting. Opponents' stacks allow for postflop play: Flash has ~59BB, Minni ~97BB. History shows aggressive play from all, but KK crushes their calling ranges.

Holding 2♥7♣ — essentially the worst starting hand. Elon opened to 300 from the button; I’m in the SB with 250 to call into a 450 pot (I’ve already posted 50). Calling requires ~35.7% equity (250/(450+250)). 2‑7 offsuit has far less equity vs a button opening range, has zero playability out of position against both opponents, and there’s no reason to 3‑bet or peel here. Preserve my ~9.7k stack for better spots — fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding J6s. The button (Elon) has raised to 300, and the Small Blind has folded. I need to call 200 more to see a flop. The pot is currently 450, meaning I am getting 2.25:1 odds (about 31% equity required). J6s is a standard defending hand in the Big Blind against a button open, as it has decent post-flop playability and flush potential. Given the price and the hand's suitedness, calling is the most profitable long-term play.

I have flopped a flush draw on an Ace-high board. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Elon), the standard play is to check to him and see how he reacts to the board. If he bets, I have enough equity with the flush draw to continue, but leading out myself would likely only get called by better hands or fold out hands I can beat later.

The flop As Ts 9h is highly coordinated, featuring a spade flush draw, open-ended straight draws (e.g., JQ, KJ), and gutshots. KK remains a strong overpair, second only to AA or sets, and blocks AA/KK/AK. Flash called a button raise from the BB preflop (loose range including Ax, suited connectors, pairs) and checked, suggesting possible weakness, draws, or air rather than a monster. With position and deep stacks (my 240bb vs. Flash's 57bb), betting thin value from weaker Ax/pairs while protecting against draws and realizing equity is optimal. Half-pot sizing (400 into 650) balances value, protection, and fold equity without overexposing to raises. Checking risks giving free cards to draws that crush us. History shows aggressive lines postflop pay off.

I have a flush draw on an Ace-high board. Elon's bet of 400 into a pot of 650 is a bit more than half-pot. To call, I need to put in 400 to win a total pot of 1450, which requires approximately 27.6% equity. A flush draw alone provides about 19% equity to hit on the turn and roughly 35% by the river. Given the potential for implied odds if I hit my flush against an Ace, calling is the most mathematically sound decision here. Raising is unnecessary as the Ace favors Elon's range, and I'd prefer to see the turn card as cheaply as possible.

I have completed a Jack-high flush on the turn with the 9s. However, the board is now paired (9s and 9h), which introduces the possibility of full houses (specifically AA, TT, A9, or T9) in Elon's range, especially given his pre-flop raise and flop continuation bet. Since I am out of position, the most prudent play is to check to the aggressor. This allows Elon to continue betting with his strong made hands or bluffs. If he bets, I can evaluate the sizing to determine if I should raise for value or just call to control the pot, keeping in mind the risk of a boat. Betting out myself might fold out his bluffs and weaker pairs that might have otherwise bet.

KK remains a strong overpair on this turn board (As Ts 9h 9s), blocking AA/AK/KQs and ahead of villain's likely calling range from BB (Ax like AQo/KQo/ATo, smaller pairs QQ/JJ, two-pair T9/A9 less likely but possible, missed draws improving to pairs). Villain checked flop and turn, showing no strong initiative, suggesting weakness or trap but more likely marginal hands/draws. Pot 1450, villain 5275 behind (~3.6 SPR), my deep stack. Betting extracts value from worse Ax/pairs that called flop, protects against river straight draws (e.g., QJ, 78) or runner-runner threats, and denies equity. 1000 (~2/3 pot) sizes well for thin value/call from worse, fold equity vs bluffs, and pot control if raised/shoved by monsters (e.g., flush, A9, sets) where I can fold profitably. Checking gives free card to improve hands we beat and allows villain to bluff river; consistent aggression maximizes EV long-term vs history of calling stations.

I have completed a Jack-high flush on the turn. However, the board has paired with the 9s, making full houses possible (AA, TT, A9, T9). Elon's bet of 1000 into 1450 is a strong sizing, suggesting he has a strong hand or is semi-bluffing. While my flush is vulnerable to a full house or a higher flush (Ks or Qs high), it is too strong to fold at this stage given the pot odds and the likelihood that Elon could be betting an Ace (AK, AQ) or a lower flush. Raising is risky because it likely only gets called by hands that beat me. Therefore, calling is the most balanced move to see the river and control the pot size while the board is paired.

On the river, the board is As-Ts-9h-9s-8c. I hold a Jack-high flush (Js-6s). While this is a very strong hand, the board is paired with nines, meaning full houses are a possibility for Elon (AA, TT, 88, or any 9 in his range like A9, T9, 89). Additionally, since I only have the Jack-high flush, I am also losing to the King-high or Queen-high flushes (Ks, Qs). By checking, I am playing a pot-control line and allowing Elon to bluff with missed straight draws or bet for thin value with an Ace. Leading out into a paired board after Elon has shown aggression on every street is risky, as it narrows his calling range to hands that mostly beat me or are very strong. Therefore, checking is the most prudent action to evaluate his river sizing.

River 8c completes some straight draws like QJ (8-9-T-J-Q) and possibly 78 (5-6-7-8-9 but unlikely), but doesn't complete the spade flush. KK remains an overpair, ahead of most Ax (AQ/AJ/AT/A9 where A9 is full house threat), smaller pairs, and bluffs in villain's BB calling range after double barrel (loose suited connectors, Ax, pairs). Paired 9s make two-pair T9 or 98 trips possible but less frequent in passive check-call line. Villain's stack 4275 into 3450 pot (SPR ~1.2) is low; checking river suggests no nuts or wants value/protection. Thin value bet extracts from worse Ax/pairs that can't fold, gets folds from air/draws, and denies equity. Sizing 2500 (~3/4 pot) balances value (call from AQ+), fold equity vs bluffs, and if shoved (all-in ~4275), profitable fold vs monsters given history of villain calling big pots but folding to aggression (e.g., Hand 2,9,15). Consistent with aggressive image built this hand and overall (won pots via pressure in 2,6,16,20,26). Checking risks chopped pot or missed value long-term.

On the river, the board is As-Ts-9h-9s-8c, and I hold a Jack-high flush. The board is paired with nines, which makes full houses a distinct possibility for Elon (especially AA, TT, A9, T9, or 88). Elon has been the aggressor on every street, and his river bet of 2500 into a 3450 pot is quite significant. However, a Jack-high flush is still an extremely strong hand in a three-handed game. While I am vulnerable to higher flushes (Ks or Qs high) and full houses, I beat all of Elon's potential bluffs and one-pair value bets like AK or AQ. Given the pot odds (roughly 2.4:1), I need to be right about 30% of the time. Folding a flush here would be too exploitable against an aggressive opponent. Raising is not advisable as it would only be called by hands that beat me. Therefore, I will call the bet to see the showdown.

Button vs two blinds in a 3-handed pot: this is the single best spot to open light. J6o is marginal but too good to fold for 100 with position and fold equity available. I want to steal the blinds while keeping the raise large enough to discourage auto-defences and to look like a legitimate opening range, but small relative to effective stacks so I can fold to a 3-bet without much pain. I will raise to 300 (3x BB). Plan: fold to a sizable 3-bet with this hand; if called I'll play postflop in position, c-betting favorable textures and giving up when I miss and face resistance.

I am holding 6s 2d in the small blind. This is a very weak hand with no significant potential. Minni raised from the button, and even with the pot odds offered, playing 6-2 offsuit out of position is a losing strategy. I will fold.

Facing a button raise to 300 in the BB with K2o, getting ~3:1 on a call of 200 into 650. K2o has some equity vs a wide button range due to the king blocker, but it's offsuit with poor postflop playability OOP. Likely dominated if king hits, and hard to realize equity on bad boards. Opponent history shows aggressive opens, but folding trash like this preserves stack for better spots. Deep stack, no need to defend wide here.

I am on the button with pocket sevens. This is a strong pocket pair in a 3-handed game, and standard strategy dictates a raise to take control of the pot and potentially end the hand pre-flop or narrow the field. I'll raise to 300 chips.

3h Jd is a trash hand offsuit with no connectivity or high card strength. Facing a button raise from Flash, who has been aggressive in previous hands, my equity is poor even against a wide range (~30-35% at best), and being OOP in SB makes postflop play difficult. Pot odds require ~26% equity to call (250 to win 700), but realization OOP is low, and J3o plays poorly postflop. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots. Deep stacks don't change this.

Facing a single-button open to 300 with KcJc in the big blind and ~97BB effective stacks. KJs is a strong heads‑up hand — I want to take the initiative rather than calling out of position. A standard 3‑bet size vs a 3BB open is roughly 3x the open; I’ll raise to 1000. That sizing gives meaningful fold equity, narrows his range, and avoids the problems of a tiny min‑raise (500) that invites too many calls.

If he folds I scoop the 450 pot. If he calls the pot will be ~2050 and effective stacks remain deep (~8.8k), so I can play postflop for value or pressure. Postflop plan: c‑bet most flops as the aggressor, use the club suit/straight potential to continue barreling on many runouts, but pot‑control and fold to heavy resistance on obvious A‑high or heavily coordinated boards. Overall 3‑bet to 1000 to isolate and take the lead rather than playing a marginal hand OOP.

I have pocket sevens on the button and opened for 300. Minni, who has shown significant aggression in previous hands (e.g., Hand #57 and Hand #13), 3-bet to 1,000 from the big blind. While 77 is vulnerable to overcards, it is strong enough to see a flop in a 3-handed game, especially with position. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and evaluate the flop, rather than 4-betting and potentially facing a shove where I'd likely be flipping or dominated.

I was the preflop 3‑bettor and this flop (Qh 3d Tc) is exactly the sort of texture I want to continuation‑bet: I have KcJc which is a strong semi‑bluff — an open‑ended straight draw (A or 9 = 8 outs), one overcard (K) and backdoor club potential — plus range advantage as the preflop aggressor. A c‑bet applies pressure to many of the hands that would call my 3‑bet (weaker broadways, small pairs, some Qx/Tx) while building the pot when I hit. I’ll size ~2/3 pot (1400) to deny correct odds to many draws and extract value from worse hands; if I get raised big I’ll fold or re‑evaluate depending on sizing and reads, but if called I plan to continue with strong equity on favorable turns (A/9 or safe cards) and pot‑control otherwise.

On a board of Qh-3d-Tc, I am facing a significant lead of 1,400 into a 2,050 pot from Minni, who 3-bet me pre-flop. My pocket sevens are now an underpair to both the Queen and the Ten. Minni's 3-bet range from the Big Blind often includes strong hands like AQ, KQ, JJ, TT, and sometimes bluffs. However, given the board texture and the size of her continuation bet, my hand is essentially a weak bluff-catcher. I have very few outs to improve (only the two remaining sevens) and will likely face further pressure on the turn and river. Against an aggressive opponent who is showing strength on a board that hits a 3-betting range well, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

84o is a complete trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and offsuit. Even on the button with position vs blinds, it's not in a standard calling or raising range preflop. Pot odds are poor (calling 100 into ~250 effective pot after call), and deep stacks mean speculative hands need better equity. Folding preserves stack for premium spots.

Heads-up, SB with 9♠7♣ — a marginal but playable hand. The BB hasn’t raised, and calling 50 more to see a flop gives excellent pot odds (risk 50 to win ~200) and keeps the pot manageable while I retain positional advantage postflop. Raising to 200 is a valid steal attempt, but against Flash (who defends widely and can 3-bet or call often) I prefer to realize equity and play the flop in position. Plan: call, evaluate the flop — continue with pairs/two pair/strong draws or use position to take the pot; otherwise fold to heavy resistance.

I am in the Big Blind with 9-6 offsuit. The Small Blind (Minni) has simply completed the bet. Since I am already in the hand and there is no additional cost to see the flop, I will check. Raising with such a weak hand is unnecessary and checking allows me to see the flop for free.

I have 9♠7♣ on Q♠ K♥ T♥ — no pair and essentially no meaningful draw (only a remote two-card straight possibility). Pot is 200 and I’m acting in position after Flash checked. With almost no equity versus his reasonable calling range and deep stacks, I should control the pot and see a turn for free rather than firing a marginal bluff that will be called by many hands. Checking preserves options: I can fold to significant aggression, float against a small probe with a plan, or try a delayed bluff if the turn is favorable. Bet here would be thin and exploitable against a wide-caller like Flash, so I check.

I have 9-high on a very coordinated board (Q-K-T). Minni checked to me. There is no reason to bluff here as this board likely hits a lot of a completion range, and I have zero showdown value or significant draws. Checking back is the most sensible play to see a free turn.

Board is Q♠ K♥ T♥ T♠; I hold 9♠7♣ — no pair, no meaningful draw. Betting here would be a thin bluff: Flash defends widely and will call most small bets with Qx/Kx/Tx/overcards, so a bet is unlikely to fold enough equity and could get me raised by a value hand. I have position (act last postflop), so pot control is best — check and see what Flash does. If he bets small I can consider a cheap call to leverage position on the river, but I’ll fold to a substantial raise. If he checks back I’ll re-evaluate the river for a delayed bluff on a favorable card; for now preserve stack and avoid bloating the pot with air.

I have 9-high on a board that is extremely coordinated (Q-K-T-T). Minni checked twice, and I checked the flop. There is no reason to bet here as I have zero equity and very little fold equity on such a board. Checking allows me to see the river for free.

Board Q♠ K♥ T♥ T♠ 8♥; I hold 9♠7♣ — no pair, no realistic draw. Both streets checked, so Flash’s range is wide but contains many hands (Qx/Kx/Tx/medium pairs/air) that will call a thin bluff. Flash has shown a tendency to defend and call down, so my fold equity on a small river bluff is low. Pot is only 200 relative to deep stacks, so there’s no reason to bloat the pot with air; pot control and capital preservation are best. I check to reach showdown (or fold if he leads out), saving chips with virtually no equity to improve.

I have 9-high on a board that is very dangerous (Q-K-T-T-8). Neither player has shown any aggression throughout the hand. While I have no showdown value, the pot is only 200 chips. There is little incentive to bluff into a board that likely pairs or connects with many hands in the opponent's range, especially since any J, 8, or heart-draw that stayed in would beat me. Checking down is the standard play here.

I’m on the button with 2♦3♣ — a very weak, non‑connected offsuit hand with poor playability.
