There are two active blinds (Flash in SB, Elon in BB) who have shown they’ll defend and get aggressive; Elon in particular is a big stack that will frequently call or 3‑bet. Raising to try to steal will often get called or re‑raised and put me in difficult post‑flop spots with almost no equity; calling (limping) for 1BB is marginal and usually negative versus their defending ranges. With 108.8BB effective and 70 hands left after this, preserving stack and waiting for a playable hand is the highest EV line. I fold pre‑flop.

I am in the Small Blind with Q7o. The button (Minni) has folded, so the action is on me. I only need to call 50 more to see a flop against Elon in the Big Blind, or I can raise to try and take the pot now. Given the shallow effective stacks and the fact that I am out of position for the rest of the hand, Q7o is a mediocre hand. However, in a blind-vs-blind situation, it has decent high-card value. I will complete the small blind to see a flop for a cheap price.

AdTc is a strong hand from the BB facing a SB limp and button fold, making it heads-up. SB limps suggest a wide, often weak range. Raising isolates the limper, takes initiative preflop, and builds the pot when likely ahead in equity. Postflop OOP vs SB is manageable due to position disadvantage for opponent. Deep stacks support a standard raise sizing to about 4x the BB or 2x the pot. Raising to 400 total is balanced and applies pressure.

I am in the Small Blind with Q7o. After I completed the blind, Elon in the Big Blind raised to 400. To call, I would need to commit another 300 chips into a total pot of 800. While I am getting decent pot odds (37.5%), Q7o is a weak hand to play out of position against a player who has shown considerable aggression and has a massive stack. Folding is the most prudent play to avoid being put in difficult spots on later streets with a marginal hand.

In a 3-handed game, K8o is a strong enough hand to open from the button. Standard strategy suggests opening almost all Kings from this position to take control of the pot and utilize positional advantage post-flop. I will raise to 300, which is a standard 3x open.

K6o is a marginal hand at best from the small blind facing a button raise to 300. The button (Flash) has a wide raising range, and my hand plays poorly post-flop out of position, especially multiway if BB defends. Pot odds are about 1.8:1 (250 to call into 450), but equity against a wide button range is insufficient considering reverse implied odds and domination risks (e.g., vs Kx better). Historical tendencies show Flash is aggressive preflop, but folding weak hands preserves stack for better spots in this long session. Standard fold.

Flash opened to 300 on the button and Elon folded, so it’s heads-up. I’m in the big blind with 5c7d — a weak, offsuit, unconnected hand that fares poorly against a button opening range and plays terribly out of position. Calling 200 into a 450 pot (~30% pot odds) requires much more equity than this hand has, and 3-betting is pointless. Fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

Preflop on the button facing the blinds (pot 150, to call 100). Holding T♣ 3♥, which is a complete trash hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card value. Standard play is to fold such weak offsuit hands preflop, even in position. No reads from history justify entering the pot with this. Preserve stack for better spots to maximize long-term EV over 150 hands.

I'm in the small blind with A♣7♦. Pot is 150 and I only need 50 to see a flop, but heads-up vs the big blind A7o is a good opening hand: it has top-pair potential and blocks some stronger A-x combos. I want initiative and fold equity rather than playing OOP passively. The table's typical open is 300, so I raise to 300 total (adding 250 more) to cut down Flash's calling range and give myself easier post‑flop decisions. If he 3-bets big I can fold or re-evaluate; if he calls I have a playable hand with deep stacks. Raise for initiative and value to 300.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 8d. Minni (Small Blind) has raised to 300, making it 200 more for me to call. 9-8 offsuit is a marginal defense against a Small Blind raise, but given the pot odds (calling 200 to win 400), it's a standard call to see a flop in position against Minni. I have a healthy stack and can play post-flop.

I have top pair A (A♣7♦) on a very dry rainbow A♠-5h-4d board. Heads‑up this is a strong hand but my kicker is middling and there are a few sets (5x/4x) that beat me. Because I opened preflop I have initiative and should protect my equity and extract thin value from worse A‑x, Kx and medium pairs. A half‑pot bet keeps weaker Ax and most worse hands in, charges the few backdoor runners, and is small enough to get called by a wide calling range. Bet 300 into the 600 pot. Plan: call a small raise and re‑evaluate on the turn; respect a large check‑raise (likely sets/two‑pair) and fold to extreme aggression unless pot odds or reads say otherwise.

On a flop of As-5h-4d, Minni has led out with a continuation bet of 300 into a 600 pot. My hand, 9c 8d, has completely missed this board, offering no straight or flush draws and only two overcards to the 5 and 4, which are weak given the Ace on board. Minni's range as the pre-flop aggressor contains many Aces and better hands. Continuing here with nothing would be a losing play long-term, as I have zero equity or viable ways to win the pot currently.

2♥4♣ is one of the weakest hands to play. Calling 100 to see a flop against the SB and BB will often leave me in a multiway pot with terrible equity and reverse implied odds; min-raising to 200 as a blind steal is too loose with this hand and likely to get 3‑bet by Elon’s large stack. With a healthy stack (11,075) I should preserve chips for profitable position opens or stronger holdings. Fold and wait for a better spot on the button.

I am in the Small Blind with Pocket Nines. The Button has folded, so the action is on me. In a blind-vs-blind situation, Pocket Nines is a premium hand. I will raise to 350 to build the pot and take the initiative against the Big Blind.

Pocket 66 is a premium hand for 3-betting against a wide SB opening range after BTN fold. SB ranges are often loose here for isolation/steal, and 66 has excellent equity vs calling range (dominates Ax, suited connectors) while having fold equity against trash. OOP but deep stacks (Flash ~86bb effective) support postflop play; we can realize equity well with sets or as overpair. Standard 3bet sizing ~3x opener (350 x 3 = 1050). Pot currently 450, this builds it nicely. Calling risks multi-street OOP playability issues; folding too tight given ~50%+ equity vs range.

I am in a blind-vs-blind situation with Pocket Nines, which is a very strong hand. After I raised to 350, the Big Blind (Elon) 3-bet to 1050. Folding is not an option given the strength of my hand. While 4-betting is a possibility, Elon has shown significant post-flop aggression in previous hands. Calling allows me to see a flop, potentially hit a set, or play a controlled pot if the board remains low. I will call the 700 to see the flop.

On a paired board of 3d-5h-5d, my pocket nines are an overpair. In a 3-bet pot where I am out of position against an aggressive opponent like Elon, checking is the standard play. This allows Elon to continue with his semi-bluffs (like AK, AQ, or suited connectors) and keeps the pot manageable. If I lead here, I often force out the hands I beat while getting called by those that have me crushed (TT-AA). I will check and see how he reacts.

Pocket 66 is an overpair on a paired 5-5-3 flop with a diamond flush draw. Flash's SB raise-call range preflop includes Ax suited (esp diamonds), broadway, pocket pairs below 66, suited connectors. This flop hits his range decently but my 66 is way ahead of Ax/o, KQo, lower pairs, and even 5x (he has one out essentially for full house). Only beaten by 55 (quads, <1%), sets (33), or unlikely runner-runner. He checks, which could be weakness or trap/set-mining, but OOP check often weak. Value bet to charge draws (diamond FD ~9 outs), worse pairs/Ax, and build pot. Half-pot check risks giving free card to overcards/draws and missing value. Sizing 1400 (~2/3 pot) balances value/protection, leaves him ~65bb behind for stacks. Deep effective ~78bb supports aggression.

I have an overpair (Pocket Nines) on a dry, paired board (3-5-5). In a 3-bet pot, Elon's range consists of premium pairs (TT-AA), which beat me, but also many overcards (AK, AQ, AJ) and potential bluffs. My hand is too strong to fold at this stage given the board texture. Raising might isolate me against only the hands that have me beat. By calling, I keep Elon's bluffs and overcards in his range and can evaluate the turn.

On the turn, the board has paired again with a third five, giving me a full house (fives full of nines). This board texture is extremely static. The only hands that realistically beat me are higher pocket pairs (TT-AA) or the case five (5c), while I am ahead of all of Elon's overcards like AK or AQ and smaller pocket pairs. Given Elon's demonstrated aggression in previous hands (e.g., Hand #49 and #51), checking is the most effective play here. It allows him to continue bluffing with his overcard range which has almost no equity against me, whereas betting would likely fold out those bluffs and only get called by hands that have me beat. I will check and plan to call a reasonably sized bet.

Turn 5s improves 66 to sixes full of fives (66655), a monster on this 5553 board. Villain's SB raise-call pre vs 3bet, check-call flop bet on 355dd (paired + FD), check turn: range Ax suited/dd (trips 555A/3), overpairs 77+ (now better FH), 3x/5x (FH/quads, unlikely exact), draws (diamond FD open). Strong value bet extracts from Ax trips/FD/weak FH (55533, low pp), overpairs may call (but beat), trap less likely OOP double check. 2/3 pot (~3300) balances value/protection vs draws, pot-committing villain (~6500 left) if calls. Checking free river to FD/brick/over improving. Long-term EV: aggression exploits villain's history of calling stations.

I have a full house (fives full of nines) on a board of 3d-5h-5d-5s. This is an extremely strong hand, but it is not the nuts, as I am vulnerable to any pocket pair higher than nines (TT, JJ, QQ, KK, AA) or the last remaining five. Elon, the Big Blind, has shown a high level of aggression in 3-bet pots throughout the game. His 3-bet pre-flop followed by significant bets on the flop and turn suggests a polarized range: either a premium overpair or a bluff with overcards like AK or AQ. If I raise here, I risk folding out his bluffs while only getting called by the overpairs that beat me. Given the stack-to-pot ratio, I am effectively committed to this hand, but calling is the superior play to keep his range wide and encourage him to fire a third barrel on the river with air. I will call the 3300 and evaluate the river.

On the river, the board is 3d-5h-5d-5s-7s and I hold pocket nines, giving me a full house (fives full of nines). This is an extremely strong hand, though I am losing to pocket pairs higher than nines (TT-AA) or the case five. Given Elon's high aggression throughout the session and the polarized nature of his 3-betting range, checking is the most profitable play. If Elon is bluffing with overcards like AK or AQ, checking allows him to shove his remaining chips into the pot, whereas leading might force him to fold. With only 3,175 chips remaining in a pot of 11,500, I am fully committed to the hand and will call any bet Elon makes, but I will check first to induce a bluff.

Monster hand with 66 for sixes full of fives (66655) on 5-5-5-3-7 board. Villain (Flash, SB) raised pre, flatted my 3bet OOP, check-called large flop bet on paired board + FD, then check-called huge turn bet when board triple-paired 5s, now checks river after bricking 7s. His range: strong Ax suited (trips 555Ax), 33 (full 55533 weaker), possible 77 (full 77555 beats us, but preflop flat OOP less common vs 3bet), unlikely 55 quads (trappy check-call OOP deep). History of Flash/opponents calling stations (e.g., Hand 76 river 2500 call, Hand 49/51 big calls). Pot 11500, villain 3175 left (~28% pot), excellent price to call. Shove all-in extracts max value from trips, weaker fulls (33), stubborn Ax/ODs. Checking risks him having showdown (we crush range), free showdown bad with nuts. Aggression exploits passive check OOP.

On a board of 3d-5h-5d-5s-7s, my pocket nines give me a full house (fives full of nines). This is a very strong hand, though I am trailing any higher pocket pair (TT-AA). Given Elon's high frequency of aggression in 3-bet pots and the massive pot odds (calling 3175 to win over 17000), folding is not an option. Elon's range in a blind-vs-blind 3-bet pot includes many air hands and overcards like AK or AQ that he might choose to bluff with given the board texture. I will call his all-in bet.

I am on the button with J7o. This is a weak hand, but I have position on both Elon and Minni. The action is currently on me as the button, and it's 100 to call since the blinds are 50/100. However, looking at the recent history, Elon and Minni are aggressive. J7o doesn't play well post-flop, and I'd rather wait for a better spot given my current stack lead from the previous hand. I will fold.

7d5c is a very weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and low card strength. Playing out of position post-flop against the BB with such trash would lead to difficult decisions and negative EV spots. While pot odds are favorable for calling (3:1), the lack of playability and equity realization OOP make folding the correct play to preserve the stack for better opportunities over 150 hands. Opponents are aggressive but this isn't a profitable steal spot with this holding.

On the button in a 3-handed pot preflop with Ts7d, a speculative offsuit hand with straight potential. Deep stacks (110x BB effective). Position is huge advantage postflop. Blinds have shown aggression but also folds in history (e.g., Flash and Minni fold to raises often). Standard button steal: high fold equity, even marginal hands like T7o profit from raising small. Raising to 3BB (300) builds pot, denies equity to calling ranges. Calling is limp bad, fold too tight. Min-raise 200 too small for fold equity.

I’m in the small blind with T♣2♠ facing a button open to 300 (pot 450, I must call 250). This hand is very weak, heavily dominated by a typical BTN opening range (lots of tens, broadways and pairs) and has poor playability out of position. Calling 250 into a 450 pot gives ~35.7% break-even equity (250/700) — T2o’s equity vs a realistic BTN open is well below that, and the big blind (Flash) still acts and may call or 3-bet, putting me in tough spots. A small reraise to 500 would be a thin squeeze with almost no fold equity versus the button and only increases variance with a hand that plays badly postflop. Fold and preserve the stack for better spots.

Elon raises to 3x from the Button. While Jack-Five suited has some playability, it is generally below the threshold for a profitable defense against a 3x open when out of position. Given Elon's recent aggression and my desire to avoid marginal spots out of position against him, I will fold this hand.

Three-handed on the button with pocket 4s and deep stacks (~110bb). This is a standard open-raise spot — I want to take the initiative, isolate the blinds and preserve my post‑flop position so I can realize set-mining implied odds or take the pot down right away. A 3x BB open is standard and gives good fold equity while not bloating the pot unnecessarily if either blind defends. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold (small pair vs a 3-bet range is often behind); if called I’m comfortable playing postflop in position and c-betting on many textures or pursuing a set when it hits. Raise to 300 total (3x BB).

I have a medium pocket pair (66) in the Small Blind. Against a Button open in a 3-handed game, this hand is very strong and is usually ahead of the opener's range. I will 3-bet to 1000 to take the initiative and attempt to win the pot immediately or isolate the Button, although I will be playing the rest of the hand out of position.

K3o is a marginal king-high hand offsuit. Facing a SB 3-bet over button open, out of position in BB. Pot odds ~2.55:1 require ~28% equity, but vs tight 3-bet range dominated by better Ks/Ax/pairs, equity <25%. Poor postflop playability OOP. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots. Opponents aggressive per history but doesn't change fundamentals.

Facing a standard-sized 3-bet to 1,000 from the SB after my 300 open. I have 4♣4♦ and ~108bb effective — this is a classic set-mining/call spot. Calling 700 more (1000 total) gives ~3:1 immediate pot odds and excellent implied odds to extract value if I hit a set, while a 4-bet would bloat the pot with a small pair and unnecessarily commit chips against a likely stronger, or polarized, 3-bet range. Flash’s 3-bet size isn’t huge and his range will include many overcards/bluffs, so I prefer to keep position and flexibility. Plan: call to see the flop in position, fold to a significant 4-bet shove, and if I miss the flop play pot control but extract maximum when I hit a set.

I am the pre-flop 3-better, and this Jack-high board is relatively favorable for my perceived range of high pairs and strong Jacks. While my pocket sixes are now an underpair to the Jack, they still hold value against her smaller pairs and non-pairing hands. I will place a continuation bet of about one-third of the pot to maintain initiative and fold out her air.

I set‑mined preflop with 4♦4♣ and called the 3‑bet to keep the pot manageable and use position. I missed the flop (5♠ J♦ 7♠) but Flash’s small continuation bet (750 into a 2850 pot) gives good pot odds to see a turn. Calling 750 requires ~20% equity; versus Flash’s 3‑bet/c‑bet range (lots of overcards, some broadway hands, and bluffs) my pocket fours have ~25–30% equity and large implied value if I hit a set. Raising here would be thin — turning a small pair into a bluff with limited fold equity and risk of a big re‑raise — so I’ll call, control the pot in position, and re‑evaluate on the turn (commit big only if I improve; fold to heavy turn shove if still behind).

The board has paired on the turn with a 5h, making the board 5s-Jd-7s-5h. My pocket sixes are now an underpair to the Jack, but they are still ahead of most of Minni's likely holdings, such as smaller pairs or straight draws. However, the board pairing the 5 is a card that doesn't significantly change the equity for either of us since it's unlikely she's holding a 5. Since I have some showdown value but don't want to bloat the pot if I am beaten by a Jack or a higher pocket pair, checking here to control the pot size and induce a bluff or see a cheap river is a prudent line.

I have 4♣4♦ on a 5♠ J♦ 7♠ 5♥ board — that’s two pair (5s and 4s). It’s a strong hand vs a 3‑bet/c‑bet range that contains many overpairs and broadways, but it’s vulnerable to any J, 7 or 5 (trips or higher two‑pair). Flash’s line (preflop 3‑bet, flop c‑bet, then checking the turn) looks like pot‑control or marginal strength rather than an obvious slowplayed monster, so I want to extract value from overpairs and worse aces/kickers while charging backdoor/spade and straight possibilities. Betting also denies a free river that could give better two‑pair/trips to villain or a completing card to unlikely draw combos. I’ll make a value/protection bet about half the pot (1,800 chips). If he check‑raises large I’ll assume trips/strong Jx and fold or re‑evaluate; if he calls I’ll re‑assess on the river.

On this Jack-high board, my pocket sixes are a marginal hand. I 3-bet pre-flop and continued on the flop, but Minni's call suggests she has a piece of the board, likely a Jack or a draw. When the turn pairs the 5 and I check, Minni's bet of 1800 into a 3600 pot represents significant strength. Given our history and the fact that she has shown a willingness to play large pots with both made hands and aggressive draws, my pocket sixes are likely behind her range of Jacks, higher pocket pairs, or even some of her semi-bluffs that still have significant equity against me. Calling here would leave me in a difficult spot on most river cards with little information. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Queen-Eight suited (Qh 8h). In a 3-handed game, this is a standard open-raising hand from the button to apply pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot with positional advantage. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate.

Pocket Queens is a premium hand, among the best pre-flop. Flash's button raise is likely wide, including many hands we dominate. As SB, 3-betting isolates potential weak callers like Minni in BB and builds the pot for value while denying equity to worse hands. Standard 3-bet size of 3x the open (900 total) balances protection and value extraction. Opponent history shows they fold to aggression often enough to make this +EV.

I have A♦8♦ in the big blind facing a button open to 300 and a small‑blind 3‑bet to 900. Effective stacks are deep (~100bb) and the 3‑bet size is relatively small, so A8s has good playability and blocker value (to AA/AK) — plenty of equity to justify a call. Folding would be too tight and 4‑betting is too thin with this hand (I don’t want to get committed out of position with a marginal hand). I’ll call the 800 to see a flop and plan to proceed based on board texture: continue aggressively if I hit an ace or strong diamond draw, otherwise play cautiously and fold to large sustained aggression.

I am on the button with Qh 8h. I opened to 300, Elon 3-bet to 900 from the small blind, and Minni called from the big blind. The pot is now 2100 and it costs me 600 to call. Although my hand is mediocre, the pot odds are 3.5:1 (600 to win a total pot that will be 2700), and I will have position throughout the hand against two opponents. Closing the action and seeing a flop with a suited semi-connected hand in position is profitable here, especially given the deep stack sizes and the likelihood of realizing equity.

Preflop 3-bet with QQ was standard aggression, building pot OOP and isolating. Flop KK6 hits opponents' calling ranges hard (any Kx, AK, broadway); QQ is likely crushed by trips or top pair but could be best vs bluffs/air. As preflop aggressor OOP vs two callers, checking protects equity, controls pot, induces bluffs from Flash (button defender), and allows Minni to lead weak. Betting risks raises from better; checking exploits history of opponents checking/folding to aggression later. Long-term EV favors pot control here on paired scary board.

Flop is K♦ K♣ 6♥. I hold A♦8♦ — ace‑high with a backdoor diamond but no pair. Elon was the preflop 3‑bettor (polarized toward KK/AA/AK/QQ+), Flash called; this is a multiway, deep‑stacked pot where a c‑bet from me has low fold equity and leaves me OOP to a caller. Elon’s check is ambiguous (pot‑control or trap), so I don’t want to inflate the pot with nothing. Plan: control the pot and gather more information. Check and fold to significant aggression; call small, non‑polarized probes selectively because my A♦ is a useful blocker to AK/AA combos, but otherwise keep the pot small and re‑evaluate on the turn (bet if I pair the A or pick up real equity).

In a three-way 3-bet pot on a paired board of 6h-Kd-Kc, I have Queen-high with a backdoor heart flush draw. The pre-flop aggressor (Elon) has checked, followed by Minni. This often indicates weakness or a desire to control the pot. While a bet might occasionally take the pot down immediately, checking back is a safer and more strategic play here. It allows me to realize my equity for free, potentially picking up a flush draw or a pair on the turn, while keeping the pot manageable against two opponents. Given the strength represented by the pre-flop action, there is a risk that one of them is trapping with a King or a high pocket pair. I will check to see the turn.

The turn Ks triples the kings on the board (Kd, Kc, Ks, 6h), transforming my QQ into kings full of queens (KKKQQ), one of the nuts here. Only beaten by the case Kh (quads) or AA (KKKAA), both unlikely given preflop calling 3-bet ranges (Flash button call wide but not AA-heavy; Minni BB call) and their flop checks behind on KK6 (where AA had KKAA two pair, Kh had trips K). Worse full houses (KKKTT through KKK66) from pocket pairs in calling ranges will call, as will weaker trips (AKo/AKs now KKK A), top pair, draws, and bluffs. Pot control not needed; betting extracts max value from 2 opponents OOP. Everyone checked flop, signaling caution/weakness. Size 2/3 pot (1800) balances value/targeting worse fullhouses/trips while denying draws equity. History shows opponents call aggression in big pots (e.g., Hand 49, 83). +EV bet builds stack long-term.

Call 1800. Elon was the preflop 3‑bettor (polarized to very strong hands) and checked the flop, so his turn lead is ambiguous — a small 1800 into 4500 looks like a probe/block or thin value bet rather than a polar shove. I hold A♦8♦: the ace is a useful blocker to AA/AK combos and I have some showdown value, but I’m out of position and can’t beat full houses/quads (or a 6 in hand). I have ~6 clean outs to improve (aces and eights), and calling 1800 to potentially win ~8100 gives me ~22% break‑even equity — acceptable against a mixed betting range. Raising is too thin OOP and folding is too tight given the price. Plan: call to see river; fold to significant river shove if I miss, consider calling a modest river bet if I pair the A but be prepared to fold to heavy action.

On a board of 6h-Kd-Kc-Ks, there are three Kings. My hand (Qh-8h) currently plays as Three of a Kind Kings with a Queen kicker (K-K-K-Q-8). However, the pre-flop action was a 3-bet by Elon (SB) and a call by Minni (BB). On this board, any pocket pair held by an opponent results in a Full House (e.g., 22 makes K-K-K-2-2), and any Ace results in a better kicker (K-K-K-A-6). Given Elon's 3-betting range and Minni's willingness to call both the 3-bet and now a turn bet of 1800, it is highly probable that at least one of them holds an Ace or a pocket pair. My Queen-high is extremely unlikely to be the best hand, and the flush draw is irrelevant because a full house or a higher kicker beats it. I have very little equity to improve to a winning hand.

River 7h is a blank that pairs no unpaired cards and completes no flush or strong straight draws (board 6h Kd Kc Ks 7h has two hearts, weak draw completion). KKKQQ is the second nuts, beaten only by quads (pocket KK unlikely post-3bet call) or AA (KKKAA aces full, possible but her flop check-call line less likely for such strength). Minni called preflop 3-bet from BB (loose range), checked flop KK6r (avoids reps with monsters), called large turn bet on triple kings (committed to full house/smaller trips/AK or bluff). History shows Minni calls aggression in big pots with marginal strength (Hands 4,41,64). OOP heads-up vs calling station, bet extracts value from worse full houses (KKKJJ/TT/etc.), AK (quads K), strong trips. Size 4200 (~2/3 pot) balances value vs her range, induces calls without overbet shoving off worse. Checking risks free showdown with our nuts; betting +EV for stack growth.

River completed K♦ K♣ K♠ 6♥ 7♥; I hold A♦8♦ — no pair and no more cards to improve. Elon was the preflop 3‑bettor (polarized toward AA/KK/AK/QQ+/strong pairs) who checked the flop and then barreled the turn and river. The turn brought the third king, which gives virtually any pocket pair in his 3‑betting range a full house (and Kx/AK quads if he holds a king). I called the 1800 turn as a priced draw/coach call given blocker value and correct pot odds then, but on the river there are zero outs. Elon’s 4200 river into the 6300 pot (pot shown 10500 after his bet) requires ~28.6% equity to call (4200 / (10500+4200)), which I do not have unless he’s bluffing. Given his line (polarized 3‑bet range, small turn probe then another bet), heads‑up situation and the board texture that turns many reasonable hands into full houses/quads, this is almost certainly value. Raising would be an extremely thin, uncredible bluff and would risk a large portion of my stack with virtually no fold equity. Correct play: fold.

3-handed on the button with T3o after SB folds and BB checks. Excellent position with initiative. T3o is playable for a steal raise: Ten is a decent blocker, hand has some straight potential, and fold equity against a checked BB range (likely weak). Dead money from SB blind adds value. Raising to 300 (3x BB) is standard size for isolation/steal. If called, I have position to realize equity postflop. Folding is too passive from BTN; limping invites trouble. Aggressive play maximizes EV long-term over 150 hands.

Elon opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the small blind with 7♠2♥ — essentially the worst hand to defend with. Calling 250 into a 450 pot (post-call pot 700) requires ~36% equity, which 7-2o simply doesn’t have versus a button opening range. Out of position, with almost no fold equity or playability and a big blind still to act (who can squeeze), this is a clear negative-EV spot. A min 3-bet bluff is possible but thin and can easily be punished. Fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

Elon raised from the button, which is a standard play for a wide range of hands. My hand, J6o, is quite weak and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position against the button. To preserve my stack and avoid unnecessary marginal spots, I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Three-handed, on the button I should be opening a very wide stealing range. 2♦6♠ is a weak hand, but in 3-handed play it's still within a standard button open frequency. Raising preserves fold equity and lets me play postflop in position; limp/call 100 would be too passive and will often see two opponents with better ranges. Flash (SB) has shown a tendency to call wide but can also be aggressive; Elon (BB) can 3-bet occasionally — I will fold to a large 3-bet with this hand. I raise to a standard ~3xBB (300) to apply pressure, stay balanced with my button opens, and maximize long-term EV. If called I'll play cautiously postflop with positional advantage; if 3-bet heavily I'll concede and fold.

I am holding a premium starting hand (AKs) in the Small Blind. Minni has raised from the button, which is a common steal attempt in 3-handed play, but regardless of her range, my hand is significantly ahead. I want to raise for value and to protect my equity by making it harder for the Big Blind (Elon) to enter the pot. A standard 3-bet size out of position is roughly 3.5x to 4x the open, so I will raise to 1100.

AKd is a premium hand, one of the best starting hands, with excellent equity, suited for flush potential, and live cards that block AA and AK. Facing a button open from Minni (likely wide range including suited connectors, broadways) and a small blind 3-bet from Flash (could be polarized but SB 3-bets vs button opens can be wide). Deep stacks allow for post-flop play, but OOP postflop as BB, I prefer to 4-bet to isolate or take it down preflop. Getting great odds to call (+pot odds), but raising applies max pressure, denies equity to worse hands, and sets up for value. Standard 4-bet size ~3x the 3-bet to 3300 total. History shows opponents aggressive but fold to pressure often (e.g., Flash folds to 3-bets/4-bets in spots). Will fold out worse, get called by sets/overpairs we dominate.

I opened wide on the button with 2♦6♠ which is fine in 3-handed play, but facing a cold 3-bet to 3,300 from Elon after Flash's reraise, this hand is dead. The current pot is 4,700 and the call is 3,000 — I would need roughly 3,000 / (4,700+3,000) ≈ 39% equity to make a cold-call profitable. 2♦6♠ has essentially no chance of getting to that equity versus a 3-bet/4-bet range of this table (Elon shows frequent strong 3-bets and Flash calls/plays wide), and I’d be out of position and likely multiway. A 4-bet bluff is available but this hand lacks meaningful blockers to high cards and would commit a large share of my stack with no playability postflop. Preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is higher EV. Fold.

I have AKs, a premier hand in 3-handed play. After Minni's open and my 3-bet, Elon has 4-bet from the Big Blind. In a 3-handed context, AKs is strong enough to play for stacks. By 5-betting all-in, I maximize fold equity against hands like JJ or TT and ensure I realize my full equity against his entire range. Given Elon's recent aggression, he could be doing this with a wide range of value hands or even some bluffs. I have him covered and am willing to take the flip or catch him overplaying a lesser hand.

AK diamond suited is a premium hand, but facing a massive 5-bet shove from Flash for effectively 13900 total (leaving him 1100 behind) after my 4-bet to 3300, this is an extremely strong range: primarily AA, KK, possibly QQ or sets, where AK is dominated or crushed. Equity vs AA ~12%, vs KK ~28%, vs QQ ~45%, but the range is nutted and unlikely to include much worse. Pot odds are about 2.65:1 (10600 to call into 28100), requiring ~27% equity, which we likely don't have against this sizing. Calling leaves us with only 925 behind into a huge pot, committed and OOP postflop even if we hit. History shows aggression, but this jam denies fold equity we had pre-4-bet and is too strong to continue profitably. Folding preserves stack for better spots in 150-hand game.

I am on the button with King-Eight offsuit in a three-handed game. This is a standard opening hand to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. I have been playing aggressively throughout the session, and with my current stack lead, I should continue to apply pressure on the shorter stacks in the blinds. I will open for my standard 3x raise.

Preflop in SB with KcTh offsuit facing a button min-raise to 300 (3BB). Pot 450, call 250 more. KTo offsuit is a marginal hand at best, lacking suitedness for playability. Button ranges are wide, but defending OOP from SB multiway potential with BB behind is tough postflop. Equity realization suffers OOP, and while we have blockers to top of range, folding avoids difficult spots and preserves stack for premium hands. History shows aggression from opponents, but no need to defend this weakly. Standard GTO calls for a fold here.

Heads-up vs a standard 3x open to 300 from the button. Pot is 450 and I need to put in 200 more (call to 300) — good pot odds (~31%) to see a flop. A♣8♦ is a strong heads-up holding with decent equity and playability, but out of position I prefer to keep the pot manageable rather than 3-bet and play a bloated pot OOP where I can be dominated by stronger Aces. I’ll call to realize equity, evaluate the flop, and proceed with a check-call/raise plan depending on texture and Flash’s aggression.

I have Ac8d on a flop of Ah 8c Tc — I’ve flopped top two pair (Aces and Eights) heads-up against the button’s wide preflop raising range. Pot is 650 and action is checked to me. This is a classic spot to bet for value and protection: I’m ahead of most Ax hands and many broadway hands, but I must charge club and straight/connector draws and deny correct odds to hands that can outdraw me. I’ll size to about 2/3–3/4 pot to maximize value and make draws pay — bet 450. If Flash check-raises a small-to-medium amount I’ll generally call to see a turn and re-evaluate (two pair is strong but vulnerable to sets/AT/T combos); if he jams or check-raises to an unmistakably huge size representing a very narrow value range I’ll re-weight towards folding given how many hands beat me (sets/TT) and avoid a marginal stack-off without more information.

I have middle pair (Eights) on a board of Ah-8c-Tc. Minni has led out with a donk bet of 450 into a 650 pot. This lead often indicates an Ace, a Ten, or a strong draw (like clubs or a straight draw). While I am behind any Ace or Ten, folding middle pair immediately on the flop in position feels too tight, especially since Minni's range for defending the big blind is wide. I will call to see the turn and evaluate based on the next card and her further action.

I hold Ac8d on Ah 8c Tc 9d — top two pair heads‑up. Flash called my sizable flop bet, so his calling range contains many Ax hands, some tens, broadway combinations and some speculative connectors. The turn 9d does create a few now‑made hands that beat me (pocket 9s, pocket 10s, and two‑card straights like JQ/J7), but those combos are a minority of his flop‑call range. Most worse Aces, many Tx and broadway hands are still behind and will pay to see another bet.

Objective is to extract value and deny free cards while keeping the pot manageable vs the small subset of hands that currently beat me. I will bet for value ~2/3 pot — large enough to charge worse Aces/Tens and price out turning into a bluff catcher on the river, but small enough not to commit my stack if he shows serious resistance. If Flash check‑raises a small/medium amount I will generally call and re‑evaluate on the river; if he check‑raises very large (an overbet or shove representing a very narrow value range) I will give up and fold to avoid getting stacked by a set/straight.

Action: bet ~2/3 pot to get value and protect — amount = 1000 chips.

On the turn 9d, the board has become increasingly coordinated (Ah-Tc-8c-9d). Minni's decision to lead out on the flop and now fire a second, larger barrel (1000 into 1550) suggests significant strength. While I have a pair of Eights, I am currently behind any Ace, any Ten, and now any Nine or Jack. The 9d also completes a straight for QJ and improves many semi-bluffs. Given Minni's betting pattern and the board texture, my middle pair is likely beat, and calling another bet out of position on such a draw-heavy board is a losing play in the long run. I will fold to preserve my stack.

27o is one of the weakest hands in poker, with no high card value, suitedness, or straight potential. Even on the button with deep stacks, entering the pot voluntarily pre-flop with this hand is -EV. Opponents like Minni and Flash have shown aggressive tendencies in previous hands, and calling the big blind risks difficult post-flop situations out of position if raised. Folding preserves my stack for better spots in this long 150-hand game.

Elon folded so it’s heads-up vs Flash (BB). I have J♦T♥ in the small blind — a strong, playable hand with good straight/broadway potential and useful blockers. In heads-up the small blind is effectively the button postflop, so I’ll have positional advantage on later streets; I want the initiative and to avoid giving Flash a cheap flop. Calling 50 is cheap and acceptable, but a standard 3BB open to 300 builds the pot when I’m likely ahead, gains fold equity, and narrows Flash’s defending range. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll typically fold this holding; if he calls I’m happy to play postflop in position. Raise to 300.

I am in the Big Blind with 7c 5h. Minni raised from the Small Blind to 300. Although the pot odds are 3-to-1 to call, 7-5 offsuit is a very weak hand and plays poorly out of position. Given Minni's frequent aggression, I don't want to waste chips on a marginal defense with a bottom-tier hand. I will fold.

3♦4♠ off is one of the weakest hands to open with. I’m on the button facing two live opponents (SB Flash and BB Elon) who defend and 3‑bet fairly often in this game, and stacks are deep (~108 BB) so I’ll get value‑raised or called frequently. A standard open is 300 here, but with offsuit 3‑4 I’m dominated by most defending ranges and have poor post‑flop playability even in position. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 9d 2h. The Button (Minni) has folded. The pot is currently 150 (100 from Elon's Big Blind and 50 from my Small Blind). It costs me 50 to call or I can raise. 92 offsuit is a very weak hand, even in a blind vs. blind scenario. While Elon has been aggressive in previous hands, I don't have enough equity with this specific hand to justify a raise or a call. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with a suited Ace (Ah2h), which is a premium opening hand in a three-handed game. I will make a standard 3x open raise to 300 to take control of the pot and leverage my positional advantage.

J6o is a marginal offsuit hand with poor connectivity and high card value that's easily dominated. Facing a button open-raise to 3BB from Flash, who tends to raise widely based on hand history, calling from SB OOP offers poor implied odds and playability. Pot odds are ~1.8:1 but equity realization is low post-flop without position. BB can squeeze or defend, but folding preserves stack for better spots in a long 150-hand game to maximize EV.

Facing a standard button open to 300. I’m in the big blind with J♠9♥ — a marginal but defendable hand heads-up. Pot is 450 and it costs me 200 more to call (total 300 committed), giving ~3.25:1 pot odds (need ≈23% equity). Against a typical wide button open J9o has enough equity and good implied odds with deep stacks. 3-betting OOP with J9o is suboptimal (min-raise has little fold equity and a big 3-bet commits too much with a marginal hand). Plan: call to see a flop, play cautiously out of position — continue with top pair/two pair/strong draws, fold to heavy aggression if I miss and face strong action.

I have top pair (9) with a good jack kicker: J♠9♥ on 9♣6♠3♠. I’m out of position and Flash’s open is wide, so he’ll c‑bet this dry board frequently. Betting OOP bloats the pot and gives up flexibility; checking lets me induce bluffs and control pot size. Plan: check and either call a standard small/medium c‑bet to realize equity and protect vs single spade cards, or check‑behind if he also shows weakness. Re‑evaluate on the turn and only commit more with clear improvement or strong reads.

I opened pre-flop with Ace-Deuce suited and was called by Minni in the Big Blind. The flop is 9c-6s-3s. While I haven't connected with this board, I have the overcard Ace and a backdoor heart flush draw. This is a standard spot for a continuation bet to take down the pot immediately, as this board is relatively dry and doesn't hit a typical calling range hard, though there are some spade draws. I'll bet roughly half the pot.

I defended preflop with J♠9♥ and on this flop (9♣6♠3♠) I have top pair with a decent jack kicker and a backdoor spade. Flash’s 325 is a standard, relatively small c‑bet on a dry board. Out of position I prefer pot control and to keep his wide c‑betting bluffs/overcards in rather than bloating the pot with a raise. Calling realizes equity, gives good pot odds (~4:1) and lets me reevaluate on the turn — I’ll lean to value/size up if I improve (two pair/trips or strong draw) and be prepared to fold to significant turn aggression on scary cards. For these reasons I will call the 325.

I hold J♠9♥ on 9♣6♠3♠-T♥ — top pair with a decent jack kicker but my backdoor spade draw is gone and the hand is fairly vulnerable. Flash is a wide button opener who c‑bet the flop and now checked the turn, which looks like pot control or weakness rather than strength. The pot is ~1,300 and my stack is deep (~10,150); betting OOP risks bloating a pot where I can easily be behind to two‑pair/trips or get raised off my hand. Prefer to control the pot and realize equity: check and go to showdown or re-evaluate on the river. If I improve (two pair/trips) or get a clear value spot on the river I’ll bet/raise; I’ll be prepared to fold to heavy river aggression representing a stronger made hand.

The turn is the Ten of Hearts, which is somewhat of a blank for my hand but could hit some of Minni's calling range (like 87, 10-9, or J10). However, I still have the overcard Ace and since Minni checked again, showing weakness, I will continue to represent a strong range. I'll bet about half the pot again to maintain pressure and try to take down the pot before the river.

I hold J♠9♥ — top pair (9) with a decent jack kicker. I defended preflop and check‑called the flop to keep Flash’s wide button c‑betting range (bluffs, overcards, spade draws) in and to control the pot. The turn (T♥) is somewhat dangerous because it brings many Tx and two‑pair combos, but Flash’s sizing (650 into a 1950 pot, roughly a third) is small and consistent with thin value and bluffs. Calling 650 gives me ~3:1 pot odds and keeps his worse hands and bluffs in; raising OOP with a single pair would bloat the pot and likely get me committed against hands that currently beat me (TT, 99, T9, sets). With deep stacks I prefer pot control and a river decision — I will call the 650 and re‑evaluate on the river. Plan on folding to a large raise or shove, and on safe runouts either check‑call small bets or extract thin value if the river clearly favors my range.

The river 6 pairs the board and I’ve improved to two pair (9s and 6s) — a solid but vulnerable holding (beaten by any 6, any Ten, and 9x with a better kicker). Flash has been opening wide and firing small c-bets on flop and turn, so his range contains a lot of missed spade draws and weaker one-pair hands that will call a modest river bet. Checking hands like this first-to-act often surrenders value and lets bluffs check through; a small value bet charges busted draws and gets called by worse while keeping the pot under control. I’ll bet ~1/3 pot to extract value and deny free showdowns: pot is 2600, so I’ll bet 900. If Flash raises big, I’ll assume he has a better made hand (T/6/strong 9) and fold; if he calls we’ll see showdown.

On the river, the board pairs with a 6. Minni, who check-called the flop and turn, suddenly leads out (donks) for 900 into a 2600 pot. This action often represents a marginal hand trying to set a price (blocking bet) or a slow-played hand that improved (like a 6). While I have Ace-high, I have no showdown value against any pair, and Minni's line is rarely a total air bluff in this specific sequence. Folding is the most prudent play as I am at the bottom of my range.

3-handed on the button, first to act pre-flop facing the big blind. Pot is 150 with blinds posted. J7o is a playable hand from this position due to excellent position post-flop, the jack high card provides some strength, and it has straight potential. Opponents have shown aggression but also folding tendencies in previous hands. Standard play is to raise for value/thin value/steal. Raising to 3x the BB (300 total) is a good size to build pot or take it down immediately. Limping is passive and exploitable; folding is too tight here. Deep stacks allow for post-flop play if called.

Elon opened to 300 from the button. I'm in the small blind with 8♣2♥ — a completely unplayable offsuit hand with no blockers and very poor postflop playability. Calling 250 to see a flop leaves me out of position against the opener (and the BB) and requires too much equity to be profitable; a min-raise to 500 is also unjustified with this holding. With a 12k stack I should conserve chips for better spots (suited connectors, pairs, broadways). Fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 5d 3d and facing a 3bb raise from Elon on the Button. The price to call is 200 more into a pot of 450, giving me 2.25:1 odds. While 53s is a weak hand, in a three-handed game against a Button open, it is a marginal defense candidate. Given the stack depths and the suitedness of the hand, I will call to see a flop and protect my big blind range.

On a flop of 6s 2d 3c, I have bottom pair (threes) with a gutshot straight draw (any 4) and a backdoor diamond draw. This is a board that likely misses a lot of the Button's opening range, while connecting reasonably well with my Big Blind defending range. Standard play here is to check to the pre-flop aggressor and evaluate their bet size. I have enough equity to continue against most standard continuation bets.

Flop is 6s-2d-3c, a very dry board with low, uncoordinated cards, minimal draws (possible 45 gutshot but rare). Rainbow, no flush threats. Pre-flop, I raised from button with J7o (positionally standard), Flash called from BB with a wide defensive range (suited connectors, Ax, pairs, etc.). Most of that range misses this flop entirely. Flash checks, showing weakness. As the pre-flop raiser, my range is perceived as strong (AJ+, pairs, suited broadways, suited connectors). J-high has showdown value but little else; however, continuation betting is standard here to charge draws, protect against overcards, extract value from weak pairs (77,66,33,22), and bluff against air. Checking gives up initiative and allows Flash to bluff turn unimpeded. With position and deep stacks, betting small-medium preserves fold equity while building pot. Size ~60% pot (400) balances value and bluffs. Opponent history shows Flash folds to aggression post-flop frequently (e.g., Hands 2,9,15,17, etc.). Goal: maximize EV by denying equity and taking it down often.

On this flop of 6s-2d-3c, I have bottom pair (threes) along with a gutshot straight draw and a backdoor diamond draw. Elon's bet of 400 into 650 is slightly larger than half pot, but my hand has significant equity against his likely continuation betting range, which contains many overcards. I need roughly 27% equity to call, and between my pair, my four outs to a straight, and potential to improve to two pair or trips, I have a comfortable continue. Raising here is unnecessary as I want to keep his bluffs in and minimize risk with a marginal hand, so I will check-call to evaluate the turn.

On the turn 9h, the board remains relatively dry. My pair of threes still holds some marginal showdown value against Elon's air (overcards), and I still have a gutshot straight draw to the wheel. Leading into the pre-flop aggressor here doesn't make much sense as I want to keep his bluffs in and I don't have a hand strong enough to value bet. I will check to see how he responds to the turn card.

Board remains relatively dry: 6s-2d-3c-9h, low uncoordinated cards with one higher card on turn and now a heart bringing a weak flush draw possibility, but still rainbow overall. My Jc7d is pure air with no pair, no draw, and minimal showdown value. Pre-flop button raise and flop c-bet 400 (called) represents a strong range including pairs, overcards, sets, and draws. Flash's BB defense range is wide but often weak post-flop; history shows frequent folds to continued aggression (e.g., Hands 2,9,15,17,53,90,93 where Flash folds to bets after calling pre/flop). Flash checks again on turn, showing continued weakness. Betting maintains initiative, charges any weak draws (e.g., 45 gutshot, heart draws now, 78/T8), extracts from trapped hands like low pairs or Ax that missed 9, and has high fold equity given opponent's tendencies. Position allows control if raised (can fold air). Checking risks free card to villain improving or bluffing river unimpeded, and pot control is less important deep-stacked with bluff potential. Bet sizing ~55% pot (800 into 1450) balances thin value/bluff, preserves stack, and maximizes fold equity without overbetting. EV positive long-term vs exploitable opponent.

On the turn 9h, the board remains quite dry, but Elon has followed up his flop continuation bet with a second, larger barrel of 800 into the 1450 pot. My hand, a pair of threes with a gutshot straight draw, is extremely marginal. While I have about 18-20% equity to improve to a straight, trips, or two pair on the river, calling 800 to win a total pot of 3050 requires roughly 26% equity to be profitable. Given that my pair of threes has very little showdown value against a button opening range that contains many overcards and better pairs, and since Elon is showing significant strength, I do not have the required odds to continue. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I’m on the button with Q♦5♦ — not a premium hand but suited and fine to open in a 3-handed pot. I want to take the initiative, steal the blinds, and avoid giving Elon (BB) a free flop. A standard 3x open to 300 offers good fold equity versus the two blinds, keeps the pot manageable and preserves post‑flop playability (backdoor diamond/queen potential). If I face a large 3‑bet (e.g. a shove or a 3‑bet to ~900+), I’ll fold; if I get callers I’ll use position to c‑bet dry flops and control the pot on wet/connected runouts.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Trey of hearts. While the hand is suited, it is very weak (T3s) to defend against an open raise from the Button, especially when out of position. Minni has been playing aggressively, and calling here would put me in a difficult spot post-flop or potentially set me up for a squeeze from Elon in the Big Blind. Given my chip lead, there is no need to play such a marginal hand out of position.

Preflop in BB facing a button raise to 300 after SB fold. Pot 450, call 200 (good odds but OOP). Hand Kd4s is weak king-high, offsuit, poor playability postflop out of position. Minni's button range is wide but still dominates this hand often (better kings, Ax, pairs). Standard GTO fold for K4o vs BTN open. Defending wider risks stack long-term; wait for better spots deepstacked.

I am holding pocket Kings on the button, which is an premium starting hand. My objective is to build the pot and extract value from my opponents. A standard 3x open raise is appropriate here to induce action or take down the blinds.

Flash raised from the button, which is a wide range in a cash game, often including suited connectors, broadway cards, and speculative hands. My ThJs is offsuit but has strong playability: two overcards, straight potential, and decent equity against a polarized button range (over 40% vs typical opens). Pot odds are excellent—calling 250 into 450 gives ~1.8:1 (requiring ~35% equity), which ThJs achieves. OOP in SB, but keeping pot small allows controlled postflop play. Folding premium equity here leaks EV long-term; 3-betting too thin without suitedness or better blockers. BB (Minni) likely defends wide, but multiway pots favor speculative hands like this. Prioritizing volume and equity realization over position concerns in deep-stack cash.

8s9s is a strong speculative hand to defend the big blind with in a 3‑way pot. Flash (button) opens to 300 and Elon (SB) calls — pot is 700 and I need to add 200 (total 300) which gives me ~22% break‑even equity. With ~120bb effective and good post‑flop playability (suited connector), calling preserves implied odds and keeps weaker hands and mono‑paired hands in. 3‑betting here would bloat a multiway pot out of position with a hand that wants to see flops; folding would be too tight given the pot odds and hand strength. Plan: call to see the flop; continue aggressively only with strong pairs or good draws, and fold to heavy action when I have little equity.

Flop board Kc5d2d is fairly dry with a weak diamond flush draw and no strong straight draws obvious, but king-high likely hits villain ranges. My ThJs has complete air: jack-high with two overcards but no pair, draw, or equity realization multiway. Pre-flop call was correct for speculative equity, but this flop massively misses. As SB first to act in 3-way pot of 900, betting J-high is suicidal against button c-bet range and BB calling range. Check to control pot size, realize fold equity if checked through, or evaluate bets. Plan to fold to any reasonable bet size, as equity <20% vs typical ranges and stacks are deep enough for villain value. Prioritizing discipline over realized dead money.

Check. I hold 9♠8♠ with no pair and no immediate draw on K♣ 5♦ 2♦ — only very weak backdoor spade/runner straight outs. This is a 3‑way pot, so my fold equity is small and a bluff c‑bet here is low EV against two opponents (one of whom opened). I prefer pot control and to preserve stack: check to see how action develops. I will fold to significant pressure, and will consider calling a single small continuation bet to realize equity, but won’t charge myself into a big multiway pot with no pair or strong draw.

I have flopped top set with pocket Kings on a board of Kc-5d-2d. This is an extremely strong hand. Although I block some top pair combinations like AK or KQ, there is a diamond flush draw on the board and plenty of other King-x hands in the opponents' ranges. Both Elon and Minni have checked, so I will place a standard continuation bet of roughly half the pot to start building value and protect against draws.

Flop Kc5d2d completely misses my ThJs: jack-high air with minimal overcard equity and a weak gutshot to Qx. Flash's button raise preflop followed by half-pot c-bet after two checks screams polarized range heavy on Kx value (KT+, K suited/diamond blockers) and diamond draws (AdXd, KdXd), both crushing my nothing. Pot odds ~2.8:1 require ~26% equity, but vs Flash's continuing range post two checks, I have <20% (overcards blocked by broadway-heavy opens, no realized draws). Minni's BB check suggests weakness, but calling risks bloating pot OOP multiway with no equity; folding preserves 11825 deep stack for better spots. Raising bluff too thin multiway against aggressive Flash (history of big cbets/value lines like Hand 96 preflop aggression). Prioritizing discipline over spew—leak control key to long-term EV in 50/100 deep cash.

Fold. I defended preflop with 9♠8♠ which is fine, but on K♣-5♦-2♦ I have no pair and only weak backdoor outs. Flash bets 500 into the 900 pot (pot now 1400) — calling 500 to win 1900 requires ~26.3% equity. My realistic equity (6 pair outs to the river, tiny runner-runner straight/flush chances) is at best around the break-even point and is likely lower against the button’s opening/c-bet range (Kx, overcards, medium pairs). I’m out of position so I have poor realization and little fold equity; raising as a bluff would bloat a pot I can’t comfortably play with this hand. Preserve stack and fold.

QTo is a strong speculative hand on the button with position on both blinds. Stacks are deep (118 BB effective), and pot odds to call are good at 1.5:1, but raising is better to take initiative, deny equity to worse hands, and isolate if called. Standard sizing for button open vs blinds is 3x BB to 300. Opponents from history (Minni aggressive raiser, Flash calls frequently) but QTo has good playability multiway if needed. Folding is too tight, limping passive. Raise to 300 builds pot when ahead or steals blinds ~33% of time.

9♥5♣ is a very weak, poorly-connected offsuit hand that is heavily dominated by a button opener. Facing a clean 3bb raise to 300, calling 250 to play out of position against both the button and the big blind is a losing long‑term play — there’s little equity, little post‑flop playability and no reason to bloat the pot. A squeeze/raise would be reckless with this holding. With a healthy stack I’ll conserve chips for better spots and fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding Qs 9d. The Button (Elon) has raised to 300. With 100 already committed and the Small Blind folding, I need to call 200 more to see the flop. Q9 offsuit is a standard defending hand in the Big Blind against a Button open, especially given the pot odds of 2.25 to 1. Elon has shown a high frequency of opening from the Button throughout the session, so his range is likely wide. I will call to see the flop.

I am out of position as the Big Blind against the pre-flop aggressor, Elon. The flop of Jc 7d 4d does not connect with my Qs 9d in any significant way. I have no pair and no real draws. Standard play here is to check to the raiser and see how he reacts. I will likely fold to any significant bet unless the turn brings me significant equity.

Flop is Jc7d4d, a coordinated board with diamond flush draw and possible straight draws (56,35,25). QTo completely misses: queen-high no pair, no straight/flush draw, gutshot nothing. However, as preflop raiser with button position, my wide range (20-30% hands) hits this ~25-30% of time (Jx,77,44,Ax,KQ suited etc.), so continuation bet maintains pressure and takes down dead money often. Flash's BB call preflop is wide (defends ~40-50%), check suggests weakness (no Jx/set/draw often checks for pot control or weakness). Deep stacks favor bluff/c-bet here; villain floats/call light but folds worse often. Small-medium bet size denies equity to draws/OESD while keeping bluffs cheap if raised. Bet 300 (~45% pot) balances value (overpairs, Jx) with bluffs like this. Checking realizes 0 equity, loses too often vs checked strength. Fold impossible.

I missed the flop completely with my Q9 offsuit. The board is Jack-high with two diamonds, and I have no pair and no real draws. Elon, the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a continuation bet. Given I have no equity here, there is no reason to put more chips into the pot. I will fold.

Short-handed on the button, I should open a very wide range. 3♦4♥ is weak but has some playability and fold equity — better to raise than limp. I’ll make the min-open to 200 to apply pressure to the blinds while keeping the pot small with a marginal hand. Plan: if the blinds fold I pick up the pot; if called I play postflop in position (c-bet good flops, control on bad ones). I will fold to a large 3-bet (or any 3-bet that’s not a small one I can reasonably call to use position). Min-raise maximizes EV here versus a simple call or limp.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Eight offsuit. Minni has opened from the Button with a min-raise to 200. While the price to call is attractive (150 to call into a pot of 350), Jack-Eight offsuit is a weak hand that performs poorly out of position. Given the three-handed nature of the game, I should be selective about the hands I play out of the blinds to avoid being squeezed or playing difficult pots post-flop. Folding is the most prudent choice here.

AJs is a premium hand against a button min-raise (200 into 350 pot). Effective stacks are deep (~11k). Button range is wide in 3-handed play based on Minni's history of frequent small raises and calls. 3-betting takes initiative, builds the pot OOP, and has good equity/post-flop playability with flush draw potential. Sizing to 600 (3x) is standard, denies equity to weaker hands, and sets up for further aggression. Calling is fine but risks multiway pots or playing OOP passively.

I opened a very wide button range with 3♦4♥ to steal the blinds, but Elon’s 3-bet to 600 is large enough that this hand has very poor equity and playability. To call I must put in 400 into a pot of ~850, requiring ~32% equity — 3♦4♥ is dominated by the sorts of pairs and broadway/Ax hands Elon will 3-bet with and has bad blocker value. A light 4‑bet to 1000 is thin here (I lack meaningful blockers and it’s easy for him to continue or jam). Preserving my ~11.6k stack and waiting for a better spot in position is the highest EV line, so I’ll fold.

I am on the button with 6-3 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. While being on the button offers a positional advantage, both Elon and Minni have shown willingness to defend their blinds and play aggressively in previous hands. Raising with such a weak hand would be an unnecessary risk with little potential for profit, so the standard and most prudent play is to fold.

Preflop from SB vs BB after button fold. K6s is a strong suited hand with good playability, straight and flush potential, and dominated only by better Ks. Excellent pot odds to call but OOP postflop. Raising steals blinds often, builds pot when ahead, and has fold equity vs BB wide range. Minni from history defends wide and calls raises frequently. Standard SB raise size 3x BB to 300 total. Can fold to 3bet if needed. EV positive long-term.

I’m in the big blind with J♥4♥ facing a single open to 300 from the small blind (button folded). This hand is marginal but suited and has backdoor flush/straight potential. I already have 100 posted so calling requires 200 more (300 total committed) and the pot odds are attractive to see a flop. I don’t want to 3-bet OOP with J4s — it’s not strong enough to build a big pot or as a reliable bluff against this opponent. Plan: call to see the flop and play cautiously postflop — continue only with a pair, good draw, or favorable texture; fold to large aggression if I miss and have no equity.

Preflop, I raised K6s from SB as a standard steal/continuation with suited broadway potential and playability. Flop 7h Ah 8c hits my range decently (A-high often, some pairs/draws) and gives me an open-ended straight draw (5 or 9 makes straight), backdoor spade flush draw, and king blocker/overcard. Board is draw-heavy (heart flush draw, straight draws like 56, 69, 9T), so Minni's BB calling range includes Ax (weak aces, suited), pairs 88/77/sets, heart draws, straight draws. As preflop raiser OOP, c-betting is standard for value with Ax in range, protection against draws, and fold equity vs his floats/misses. He defends wide per history (calls raises frequently, limps pots, chases draws), but first to act and pot odds pressure him. Half-pot bet builds pot when ahead, charges draws, denies equity. Larger risks overbet but half-pot balanced. Effective stacks deep (~12bb? Wait no, 12125/100=121BB deep), so multi-street plan viable. Check-fold too weak with equity/draw; check-raise traps less. Bet 400 (2/3 pot) optimal size for fold equity/value.

Heads-up on a three-card flop (7♥ A♥ 8♣). I have J♥4♥ — a strong two‑card heart draw plus backdoor pair outs. Elon’s bet is 400 into a 1000 pot, so calling 400 gets me pot odds of 400/(1000+400) ≈ 28.6% to see the next card. On the turn I have 9 hearts + 6 pair cards = 15 outs (15/47 ≈ 31.9% to improve on the turn); two‑card equity to the river to make a flush or pair is ~54%. Given these numbers and the stacks, folding would be too tight and a raise (min to 800) would needlessly inflate the pot OOP with only a draw against an opponent who calls a lot. I’ll call to realize my equity, keep the pot manageable, and re‑evaluate on the turn — raise or shove only if I hit a strong improvement or he shows clear weakness.

On the turn, the board is 7h Ah 8c 2d. I have Ks6s: king-high with no pair, no straight draw (earlier flop analysis overstated equity; it's gutshot at best to 9 but unrealistic), and backdoor spade flush draw. Preflop raise and flop cbet was standard with suited king for range advantage and semi-bluff potential on a draw-heavy A-high board (hearts, OESD like 56/69/9T). Minni's flop call from BB suggests Ax (weak A), mid pairs (77/88), sets, heart draws, or straight draws, given his history of wide defense, calling raises, and chasing pots (e.g., Hands 1,4,8,13,41). Turn 2d is a low brick: doesn't complete flush draws heavily (diamonds introduce backdoor but minor), improves few hands significantly, but keeps board scary. Pot 1400, OOP first to act, stacks deep (~117BB/109BB effective). Betting king-high is a pure bluff, reps strong Ax/sets, but risks raise from his value range while folding out bluffs/misses I beat; sizing would need to be committed-ish (e.g., 1000+), poor EV OOP multiway potential. Check preserves stack, induces bluffs from his missed draws (which he chases), allows cheap fold to value bets (standard vs his calling range), and realizes any thin equity vs air. No reason to bloat pot with marginal hand. Plan: check-fold to aggression or check-call small if very cheap, but expect fold to most bets.

Turn decision: Elon checked to me on the 7♥ A♥ 8♣ 2♦ board. I hold J♥4♥ — I have a strong draw: 9 hearts to make a flush and 6 additional pair-outs (J or 4) for a total of 15 outs, so ~15/46 ≈ 32.6% to improve to at least a pair or flush on the river. Against an Ace-heavy calling/raising range my realistic 'clean' outs are just the 9 hearts (9/46 ≈ 19.6%), so I can’t rely purely on seeing a free river. Elon’s check can indicate weakness or pot control, so I should try to leverage fold equity instead of giving him a free card. A half-pot semi-bluff (~700 into 1400) achieves three things: (1) it folds out many hands that currently beat me but have little showdown value (overcards, small pairs, KQ, etc.), (2) it charges any non-heart draws, and (3) if called I still have decent equity to improve on the river and keep the pot at a manageable size relative to stacks. Plan: bet 700 as a semi-bluff; if he folds I take the pot, if he calls I evaluate river and barrel for value if I hit or sometimes bluff-catch depending on river and his action; if he raises large I will respect it and fold this non-made hand.

Turn board 2d is a relatively blank card that doesn't heavily complete draws (no heart or straight completion beyond backdoors) and keeps my king-high live with a blocker to strong Ax hands like AK/AQ. Minni's BB calling range preflop and flop c-bet on draw-heavy 789A rainbow-ish board includes weak Ax (AJ-AT, suited), pairs like 77/88, sets, heart draws (Jx, Tx suited hearts), and straight draws (56,69,9T). His check-raise sizing of 700 into 1400 (now pot 2100, 1/3 pot) after my check is small and polarizing: thin value with Ax/top pair or bluffs/missed draws, per his history of wide defense, calling stations (e.g., Hands 1,4,8,13,21,40,41,44,54,67,70,74,79,90), and occasional aggression on turns/rivers when ahead or semi-bluffing. Pot odds excellent at 700 to win 2800 (~3.2:1, need ~24% equity), and K-high has ~25-35% vs his likely range (beats bluffs/air/weak pairs below K, some draws; loses to Ax/sets but blocks AK). Deep stacks (~110BB effective) allow check-call thin OOP for pot control, realize equity, and induce further bluffs or cheap showdown. Raising thin bluff reps too strong OOP and risks stack-off vs value; folding too tight given price and blockers. Plan: call, evaluate river (fold to strong value, value bet/ check thin showdown if checked to). EV+ long-term balancing range.

River board pairs deuces (7h Ah 8c 2d 2c), a low completion that improves pocket 2s to boats, some Ax/2x to two pair, but mostly a blank for strong hands. Preflop SB raise with K6s suited (playable, steal range), flop c-bet standard on drawy A-high board reps Ax/sets/draws. Minni BB call flop wide per history (loose passive defender, chases pots: e.g., Hands 1,4,8,13,21,40,41,44,54,67,70,74,79,90). Turn check by me (marginal K-high, OOP pot control), his 700 bet (~1/3 pot into 1400) after check polarizing: thin value (Ax, 77/88), delayed draws/semis, or floats; my call realizes equity (~25-30% vs range) with pot odds/K blocker. River OOP first to act with K-high (beats bluffs/air/weak pairs <K, 25-40% vs villain range excluding nuts). Betting pure bluff risky: reps boats/sets/A2 but OOP vs sticky calling station, low fold equity, commits further stacks (~110BB deep). Check optimal: pot control OOP, induces bluffs from missed draws/floats (villain history chases but bets turn weakly), cheap showdown vs weak Ax/pairs, fold to value aggression. Balanced as check-call/raise range includes value. EV+ long-term.

River: board 7♥ A♥ 8♣ 2♦ 2♣; I hold J♥4♥. I completely missed — no pair and the heart didn’t come. My turn raise was a semi‑bluff and Elon’s call shows medium strength (top pair or a pair of the board cards) rather than folding-ready air. On the river the board pairing increases the likelihood he has a callable made hand (A‑x, an 8, or at least a showdown‑worthy holding) and his check is consistent with pot control or a slow‑played value hand. To make a profitable bluff I’d need a fairly high fold frequency (B/(P+B)); even a small bet needs >20% folds, which I don’t expect given he called my turn raise. Betting risks a call or a raise where I have no redraw and would have to fold. Long‑term EV is higher preserving my stack here, so I check back and take the showdown rather than bluffing a thin spot.

3-handed pre-flop on the button facing a 100 BB to call into a 150 pot with 2c9h, an offsuit low-card gapped hand with no playability. This is a clear fold as it has very low equity against any reasonable range and poor post-flop potential even in position. Preserves stack for better spots.

Heads‑up in the blinds with pocket 7s. This is a strong heads‑up holding and I don’t want to be playing OOP for free too often — raising takes the initiative, extracts value from worse hands, and has fold equity against a wide BB defense. A standard 3x BB total (300) is a good balance: it forces Flash to call +200 to see a flop and builds the pot to about 600 if called, but isn't oversized given deep stacks. If he 3‑bets large I can fold and wait for a better spot; if he calls I’ll C‑bet favorable flops and look to set‑mine or extract value. Raise to 300.

I am in the Big Blind with a pocket pair (4s). Minni raised to 300 from the Small Blind. In a heads-up situation where the Button has folded, I have position on the Small Blind post-flop. Pocket Fours are a solid hand to defend with here; folding would be too tight, and raising might unnecessarily bloat the pot with a vulnerable pair. Calling allows me to see a flop and utilize my positional advantage.

Flop: K-6-3 rainbow. I hold 7♠7♥ — a medium pocket pair that’s ahead of a lot of Flash’s flatting range (small pairs, many overcards, suited connectors) but behind Kx, overpairs and sets. The board is relatively dry (no flush, only thin straight outs like 4‑5). I have initiative and should C‑bet to get value from worse hands, deny equity to the few draws, and fold out some overcards. I’ll size for ~2/3 pot to give good fold equity and extract value from calls (small pairs, weaker Kx, A‑x). If called I’ll proceed cautiously on turns that improve him (a King or overcard) and look to pot‑control; if he raises big I’ll respect that range and fold more often. Bet 400 into 600 pot (~2/3 pot).

On this King-high flop, Minni's continuation bet of 400 into a 600 pot (making it 1000 total) is a standard move. My pocket fours are currently a marginal middle-strength hand. While I have a small pair, the King is a very strong card for the pre-flop raiser's range. However, I have a positional advantage and the board is relatively dry (no obvious straight or flush draws). Folding immediately feels too weak against a single bet, especially since I can still improve to a set or potentially win if the turn and river go check-check or if Minni is bluffing with air. I will call the 400 to see a turn card and evaluate Minni's further aggression.

I have pocket 7s on a K‑6‑3‑T board after villain called my 400 c‑bet on the flop. The turn Ten significantly improves or pairs many hands in his flatting range (T‑x combos, Kx, some A‑x that now have showdown value) and he checked it, which is consistent with a range that either already beats me or wants pot control. I’m out of position and have limited fold equity with a medium pair against his wide call range, so I don’t want to inflate the pot when I’m often behind. Checking preserves pot control, allows me to see a river cheaply, and keeps the option to either realize showdown value against worse hands or induce/bluff on the river if he gives a free card. Plan: check the turn, then re‑evaluate on the river — call small bets, fold to large aggression, or fire a thin value bet on a harmless river if he checks.

On the turn, the board now shows 6c 3s Kh Tc. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor who continuation-bet the flop, has now checked. My pocket fours are currently a weak pair, and the Ten is another overcard that makes my hand even more vulnerable. However, Minni's check often indicates a desire to go to showdown cheaply with a marginal hand or a complete giveaway with air. Betting here doesn't achieve much for value, as I'm unlikely to be called by worse hands, and I'm not strong enough to turn my hand into a bluff to push her off a King. Checking back allows me to realize my equity and potentially win at showdown if the river remains passive.

I check. The Q♣ on the river is a dangerous card — it pairs Queens and completes club flush possibilities — and given Flash’s line (called my 2/3 c‑bet on the K‑6‑3 flop then checked the T turn) his range is weighted toward Kx, Tx, Qx and medium pairs that now beat my 7s. A river bet here would only get thin value from a very small subset of worse hands (small pocket pairs) while getting called or raised by most hands that beat me (K/Q/T, straights, any completed club flush). I pot‑controlled the turn for exactly this reason; with 9550 behind and a 1400 pot I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Check and take showdown (and plan to call small river bets but fold to large raises).

The board has run out 6c 3s Kh Tc Qc, which is quite coordinated with several overcards to my pocket fours. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, bet the flop but has checked both the turn and the river. While her checks suggest weakness or a marginal showdown hand, my pocket fours have almost no showdown value on this board, as even most of her 'air' (like Ace-high) now beats me. However, betting as a bluff is risky because the board contains many cards that hit a pre-flop raiser's range (K, Q, T), and she might check-call with a mediocre hand. Checking back is the safest option to reach showdown without committing more chips with a hand that is likely beaten.

I’ll open from the button with 5♣6♠ to seize initiative and apply fold equity. 3x BB (raise to 300) is a standard steal size here — large enough to discourage easy defense but not so large it invites a big 3-bet. With ~110bb effective stacks this hand has some postflop playability in position versus a single caller; plan is to fold to a sizable 3-bet, call small or single blind calls and use position to c-bet favorable flops, and play very cautiously if it becomes multiway. Raising maximizes expected value vs the blinds and preserves options postflop.

Holding Ace-Nine offsuit in the Small Blind against a Button open, this is a standard 3-betting situation in short-handed play to isolate the opener and take control of the hand. Calling would put me out of position for the rest of the hand without the initiative. A 3-bet to 1000 is a standard sizing to put pressure on Minni's button range.

75o is a trash hand with no playability. Facing a button open and SB 3-bet, which is likely strong. OOP postflop would be disastrous even if I flop something. Pot odds are about 1.5:1 but no implied odds with this hand. Opponents have shown aggression throughout history, especially Flash. Best to fold and wait for better spots to maximize stack long-term.

I opened to 300 on the button with 5♣6♠ and Flash (SB) 3‑bet to 1000. The pot is 1400 and it only costs me 700 to call, which means I need ~33% equity (700/(1400+700)). 5‑6 offsuit has well under that equity versus a typical SB 3‑bet range and has poor postflop playability as an offsuit connector against a likely strong range. A 4‑bet bluff is an option in some spots, but here Flash’s sizing and tendencies make that line too risky given stacks. Consistent with my preflop plan to fold to a sizable 3‑bet, I fold and preserve chips for better, more +EV situations.

I am on the button with A2o in a 3-handed game. An Ace-high hand, even with a weak kicker, is a standard opening hand from the Button to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. I have been opening for 300 (3x) consistently throughout the match and will maintain that sizing to exert pressure on the blinds.

Pocket kings is the second-best starting hand in NLHE, crushing the button's wide raising range which likely includes many speculative hands, broadways, suited connectors, and Ax.
